proposals.title: When Pretraining Fails: Quantifying and Mitigating the Cost of Suboptimal Features in Transfer Learning
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in deep learning relies on transferring features from large, generic pretrained models (e.g., CLIP, BERT, ResNet) to downstream tasks. This assumes these features are universally beneficial. However, in practice, pretraining data may be misaligned, contain biases, or lack crucial concepts for a specific domain, leading to suboptimal or corrupted features. The systematic trade-offs and failure modes when this assumption breaks are poorly understood, posing a risk to reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the performance of fine-tuning on a downstream task degrades in a predictable, non-linear manner as the semantic alignment between pretrained features and the task decreases, and that this degradation can be mitigated by selectively discarding or recalibrating low-confidence features based on the model's own uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will construct a controlled benchmark by taking a high-quality pretrained vision or language model (e.g., CLIP-ViT) and systematically corrupting its feature space via targeted noise injection, adversarial perturbations, and dataset shift simulations. We will then analyze fine-tuning dynamics and final performance across these conditions. Building on Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation, we will develop a 'confidence-gated fine-tuning' method that uses the model's layer-wise confidence preferences to identify and down-weight suboptimal features during adaptation, comparing it to standard full fine-tuning and linear probing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous framework for diagnosing feature transferability failures, moving beyond the assumption that pretrained features are always useful. It will yield a practical, confidence-based algorithm for robust adaptation when pretraining is imperfect. The findings will inform best practices for transfer learning in non-ideal scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While most work focuses on maximizing transfer from good features, we systematically study the under-explored regime of suboptimal features. Our mitigation strategy is novel in leveraging the model's internal confidence estimates—not external data—to guide the fine-tuning process, connecting uncertainty quantification with robust adaptation.

proposals.title: When Fine-Tuning Outperforms Linear Probing Out-of-Distribution: A Study of Feature Confounders and Confidence-Guided Adaptation
proposals.motivation: A dominant pre-2026 finding in transfer learning is that linear probing on frozen, high-quality pretrained features often yields superior out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness compared to full fine-tuning, which is thought to discard pretrained knowledge. This has led to a widespread preference for linear evaluation. However, this relationship is treated as a universal law, potentially overlooking scenarios where fine-tuning's greater adaptability is beneficial. Identifying these conditions is crucial for developing more nuanced and effective adaptation strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-tuning will outperform linear probing OOD when the pretrained features contain spurious correlations or 'confounders' specific to the source data distribution, and when the fine-tuning process is regularized using model confidence to selectively preserve robust, core features while adapting superficial ones.
proposals.method: We will systematically construct datasets where pretrained vision or language models exhibit high in-distribution accuracy but encode known, measurable confounders (e.g., background textures in images, syntactic shortcuts in text). We will compare OOD performance of linear probing versus fine-tuning under controlled distribution shifts. Crucially, we will develop a confidence-guided fine-tuning method, inspired by Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation, which uses the model's own confidence signals to down-weight updates to low-entropy, core feature representations while allowing larger updates to high-entropy, spurious ones. Experiments will use established pre-2026 architectures (e.g., CLIP, BERT) and OOD benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more granular understanding of the fine-tuning vs. linear probing trade-off, moving beyond a simple rule to a conditional framework. It will introduce a novel, confidence-regularized fine-tuning algorithm designed for OOD robustness. The findings will guide practitioners in selecting adaptation strategies based on diagnosable properties of their pretrained features and target domain.
proposals.novelty: Contrary to the prevailing narrative that linear probing is generally safer for OOD performance, we seek to invert this relationship and formally characterize the conditions where full fine-tuning is superior. The integration of explicit confidence estimation into the fine-tuning objective to guide selective feature adaptation is a novel methodological approach to this problem.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Analysis of Feature Distortion: How Fine-Tuning Degrades Pretrained Representations
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning is the dominant paradigm for adapting large pretrained models, yet empirical studies pre-2026 show it can degrade robust, general-purpose features learned during pretraining, leading to poor out-of-distribution performance and catastrophic forgetting. Understanding the specific mechanisms of this distortion is crucial for developing more stable and reliable adaptation methods, especially for safety-critical applications where preserving pretrained knowledge is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-tuning induces feature distortion primarily through two mechanisms: (1) the amplification of task-specific, non-robust features that act as 'shortcuts' for the target dataset, and (2) the misalignment of gradient updates that disproportionately affect high-confidence, low-entropy feature directions established during pretraining.
proposals.method: We will conduct a layer-wise and neuron-level analysis of feature evolution in vision transformers (ViTs) and large language models (LLMs) during fine-tuning. Using centered kernel alignment (CKA) and linear probes, we will track representational similarity and task-specific utility. We will adapt the 'confidence preferences' framework from Kumar's prior work to identify which pretrained features are most vulnerable to distortion. Controlled experiments will compare standard fine-tuning against methods like linear probing, LoRA, and bias-term tuning to isolate the impact of different parameter updates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, causal understanding of *how* and *why* features distort during fine-tuning, moving beyond correlational observations. It will yield practical guidelines for diagnosing and mitigating distortion, contributing to more sample-efficient and robust adaptation. The findings will bridge research on shortcut learning, catastrophic forgetting, and representation geometry.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes performance degradation, a mechanistic, diagnostic analysis of the specific *process* of feature distortion—linking it to confidence metrics and shortcut learning—is novel. This approach integrates the researcher's expertise in confidence estimation with a fine-grained representational analysis not commonly applied to the fine-tuning problem.

proposals.title: Feature Distortion in Few-Shot Fine-Tuning: Diagnosing and Mitigating Overfitting via Relative Confidence
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large pre-trained models on small datasets is a critical but brittle component of modern NLP, yet the precise mechanisms of failure in low-data regimes are poorly understood. Pre-2026, we know fine-tuning can distort useful pre-trained features, but the trade-off between adaptation and catastrophic forgetting is most acute in few-shot settings, where overfitting risks are highest. This work aims to systematically characterize this trade-off to enable more reliable and efficient adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in few-shot fine-tuning, standard gradient-based updates cause severe and disproportionate distortion of a model's general-purpose feature representations, which can be diagnosed by monitoring the model's relative confidence in its pre-trained knowledge versus new task data.
proposals.method: We will design controlled few-shot fine-tuning experiments on language models (e.g., T5 or GPT-2 variants) across diverse NLP tasks. To quantify feature distortion, we will track changes in internal representations (e.g., CKA similarity) and model confidence. Building on Kumar's prior work, we will employ a 'Confidence Preference' probe to measure the model's relative confidence between its original pre-training distribution and the new fine-tuning examples. We will then test mitigation strategies, including confidence-regularized fine-tuning and selective layer freezing, to preserve core features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a diagnostic framework for understanding overfitting in few-shot learning beyond just validation loss. It will yield practical, confidence-based guidelines for when and how to fine-tune in data-scarce scenarios. The findings will advance the goal of building robust, data-efficient learners that retain generalizable knowledge.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot fine-tuning is widely studied, our approach uniquely links the phenomenon of feature distortion to quantifiable shifts in a model's relative confidence, offering a new diagnostic lens. Combining representation analysis with confidence preferences to guide regularization is a novel methodological synthesis.

proposals.title: Beyond Surgical Adaptation: When Does Fine-Tuning on Large Target Datasets Outperform Full Fine-Tuning?
proposals.motivation: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA are widely adopted under the assumption of small, expensive target datasets, where they prevent catastrophic forgetting. However, with the rise of large, curated public datasets for specialized tasks, the core assumption of adaptation data scarcity is inverted. It is unknown whether the benefits of surgical tuning—computational efficiency and knowledge preservation—persist or become liabilities when abundant in-domain data is available.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for large, high-quality target datasets, full fine-tuning will eventually outperform surgical PEFT methods on in-distribution performance, but that a novel confidence-based selective tuning approach, which surgically updates only low-confidence model components, can match or exceed full fine-tuning's accuracy while retaining greater robustness and computational efficiency.
proposals.method: We will systematically compare full fine-tuning against LoRA and (IA)^3 on a spectrum of target dataset sizes using the FLAN collection and specialized corpora like PubMed. Building on Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation, we will develop a 'Confidence-Guided Surgical Tuning' (CGST) algorithm. CGST will use the model's per-layer confidence preferences on a validation split to identify and fully update only parameters associated with low-confidence predictions, while keeping high-confidence, foundational knowledge frozen via a sparsity-inducing mask.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic framework for choosing a tuning strategy based on target dataset scale and quality, moving beyond the one-size-fits-all PEFT paradigm. It will contribute a new algorithm, CGST, that dynamically adjusts tuning capacity, and offer insights into the relationship between dataset size, model confidence, and knowledge consolidation in adaptation.
proposals.novelty: The study directly challenges the foundational small-data premise of surgical tuning. The novelty lies in using the model's own confidence estimates—not just parameter efficiency—to decide what to tune, creating a hybrid strategy that is neither purely surgical nor fully dense.

proposals.title: Meta-Learned Tuning Policies for Robust Adaptation to Distribution Shifts
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large pre-trained models is the dominant paradigm for adaptation, but standard approaches like full fine-tuning or linear probing are suboptimal. They either overfit to small target data or underfit by ignoring crucial higher-layer features, leading to poor and inconsistent performance across the diverse distribution shifts encountered in real-world applications. A principled, automated method for selecting which layers to tune for a given shift is a critical unsolved problem.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning algorithm can learn a generalizable policy for layer-wise tuning selection that, conditioned on a dataset's characteristics, outperforms fixed tuning strategies across a distribution of related domain shifts.
proposals.method: We will frame optimal layer selection as a meta-learning problem. Using a distribution of related tasks (e.g., shifts across image styles, text genres, or noise types), we will train a lightweight hypernetwork or policy network (e.g., a small transformer or MLP) via gradient-based meta-learning (e.g., MAML or Reptile). This meta-learner will take as input dataset statistics (e.g., gradient norms, feature similarity to source) and output a soft mask over model layers, dictating their learning rates. The outer-loop meta-objective will be to maximize average validation performance across the task distribution after inner-loop adaptation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven, automated alternative to heuristic tuning strategies, improving robustness and efficiency in transfer learning. It will contribute a new perspective on adaptation by learning *how to adapt* from experience with related shifts, directly linking to Ananya Kumar's themes of confidence and distribution. The resulting tuning policy will be a compact, reusable module for efficient deployment.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been used for hyperparameter optimization, applying it specifically to learn a conditional, layer-wise *tuning policy* for handling distribution shifts is novel. This moves beyond static layer selection or uniform fine-tuning to a context-aware adaptation strategy.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Domain Adaptation via Contrastive Pre-training on Unlabeled Source and Target Data
proposals.motivation: Domain adaptation is crucial for deploying models in real-world settings, but it traditionally relies on labeled source data, which is expensive and privacy-sensitive to obtain. Recent advances in contrastive self-supervised learning (e.g., SimCLR, MoCo) have shown remarkable success in learning transferable representations from unlabeled data. This project investigates whether we can eliminate the need for labeled source data entirely, addressing a key bottleneck in scalable and privacy-preserving machine learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model pre-trained with a contrastive objective on a mixture of unlabeled source and target data will learn domain-invariant features that, when fine-tuned on a small amount of labeled target data, will match or exceed the performance of models adapted from a labeled source pre-training.
proposals.method: We will design a two-stage framework. First, we will pre-train a vision transformer (ViT) or ResNet encoder using a contrastive loss (e.g., InfoNCE) on a combined pool of unlabeled source and target images, employing strong augmentations. Crucially, we will experiment with domain-aware negative sampling strategies to encourage domain-agnostic feature learning. Second, we will evaluate the learned representations by fine-tuning the encoder on a limited set of labeled target data across standard benchmarks (e.g., Office-Home, VisDA-C). We will compare against supervised source pre-training and standard self-supervised baselines.
proposals.contribution: This work would demonstrate a new, label-efficient paradigm for domain adaptation, reducing dependency on annotated source datasets. It would provide empirical evidence and methodological insights into learning transferable features directly from unlabeled multi-domain data, contributing to both the domain adaptation and self-supervised learning communities.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive pre-training is established, its application to domain adaptation has focused on target-only or source-only unlabeled data. The non-obvious aspect is rigorously testing if mixing unlabeled domains during pre-training, without any source labels, can effectively replace supervised source pre-training for adaptation, challenging a core assumption in the field.

proposals.title: Confidence-Guided Partial and Open-Set Domain Adaptation via Relative Preference Learning
proposals.motivation: Prior work on domain adaptation, including studies on model confidence, often assumes a shared label space between source and target domains. This assumption is frequently violated in real-world applications where the target domain may contain only a subset of source classes (partial DA) or introduce novel, unseen classes (open-set DA). Addressing this mismatch is crucial for deploying robust models in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's relative confidence preferences, as explored in prior work for in-distribution calibration, can be leveraged to identify and correctly handle samples from shared, private-source, and novel-target classes, enabling more effective adaptation under label space mismatch.
proposals.method: We will extend the relative confidence estimation framework from 'Language Models Prefer What They Know' to the visual domain for partial/open-set DA. Building on architectures like Vision Transformers (ViTs), we will design a multi-branch confidence head that, alongside a standard classifier, estimates the model's preference for its prediction versus a calibrated reference. This signal will guide a tailored training objective with weighted losses for known-class alignment, entropy minimization for shared classes, and entropy maximization for detected outlier samples. We will benchmark on standard partial DA (Office-Home) and open-set DA (Office-31) datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, confidence-based method for practical domain adaptation with non-identical label spaces. It will yield new insights into how a model's internal confidence signals correlate with domain and category shift, moving beyond the restrictive shared label space assumption.
proposals.novelty: While confidence has been used for domain alignment, its application via relative preference learning to dynamically discriminate between shared, source-private, and target-novel categories in a unified framework is novel. This bridges the gap between the researcher's expertise in confidence estimation and the practical challenge of open-set/partial DA.

proposals.title: Reverse Domain Adaptation: Leveraging Labeled Target Data to Structure Unlabeled Source Domains
proposals.motivation: Standard unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) assumes a labeled source and unlabeled target, but many real-world scenarios invert this: a small, curated labeled dataset exists for a target task (e.g., a proprietary medical image set), while vast, unlabeled, and potentially noisier data is available from a broader source domain (e.g., general web images). Pre-2026, methods to effectively leverage labeled target data to guide learning from a large, unlabeled source remain underexplored, limiting the use of abundant but messy external data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's relative confidence on labeled target data can be used to generate a training signal for the unlabeled source domain, effectively inverting the adaptation direction to improve generalization and robustness by structuring the source representation space.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Confidence Preferences' framework from Kumar's prior work. A base model (e.g., a vision transformer or CNN) will be fine-tuned on the small, labeled target dataset. This model will then generate pseudo-labels for the unlabeled source data, but instead of using raw logits, we will employ a confidence ranking loss. Specifically, we will enforce that the model's confidence on correctly classified target examples is higher than its confidence on source pseudo-labels, creating a preference ordering. This signal will be used to regularize the feature extractor when training on the source pseudo-labels, preventing overfitting to source noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will formalize the 'reverse UDA' problem setting and provide a novel, confidence-based algorithm for it. It will demonstrate how small, high-quality labeled datasets can act as anchors to safely harness large, uncurated source data, expanding the applicability of domain adaptation. We will validate contributions on standard domain adaptation benchmarks (e.g., Office-Home, DomainNet) under the reversed label configuration.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is inverting the standard UDA label assumption and using target-derived confidence preferences as the primary learning signal for the unlabeled source, contrasting with typical source-to-target adaptation or semi-supervised learning methods.

proposals.title: Relative Confidence for Multi-Source and Multi-Target Adaptation: Extending Generative Classifiers Beyond the Two-Domain Paradigm
proposals.motivation: Most theoretical and practical work in domain adaptation (DA) simplifies the problem to a single source and target domain. However, real-world applications, such as deploying a model across diverse geographic regions or adapting to multiple sensor types, inherently involve multiple sources or targets. Pre-2026, the theoretical underpinnings for such multi-domain scenarios, particularly concerning model confidence and shortcut learning, remain underdeveloped, limiting robust deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the principles of relative confidence estimation and generative classifiers to multi-domain settings will provide a more robust theoretical framework, enabling models to avoid spurious correlations and select reliable knowledge from multiple sources for adaptation to multiple targets.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Confidence Preferences' framework to compute pairwise relative confidence scores across multiple domains. For multi-source adaptation, we will design a gating mechanism, informed by these confidence scores, to weight contributions from different source domains during training on a single target. For multi-target adaptation, we will adapt the generative classifier framework to learn a shared, robust representation that can be specialized via lightweight, target-specific heads, regularized by inter-target confidence disparities to prevent shortcut solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical and algorithmic extension of confidence-based and generative DA methods to realistic multi-domain scenarios. It will deliver a practical toolkit for robust adaptation in complex, heterogeneous environments, moving the field beyond the restrictive two-domain setup.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally integrating relative confidence estimation—a concept developed for model calibration and two-domain DA—into the structurally distinct problems of multi-source and multi-target adaptation, creating a unified confidence-aware framework for these settings.

proposals.title: A Unified Theory of Confidence and Generalization for Diverse Self-Supervised Pre-Training Objectives
proposals.motivation: Recent theoretical work has provided insights into the generalization and feature learning of contrastive self-supervised learning (SSL). However, the landscape of SSL is diverse, including methods like masked autoencoding (MAE), diffusion models, and clustering-based approaches (e.g., SwAV). A unified theoretical framework explaining how these different pre-training objectives shape model confidence, avoid shortcuts, and influence downstream task performance is critically lacking. This gap limits our ability to design robust, general-purpose pre-training strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core theoretical principle—linking the implicit regularization of the pre-training objective to the model's confidence calibration and its resistance to shortcut learning—can be generalized across major SSL families, providing predictive power for downstream task performance.
proposals.method: We will extend the theoretical tools from Dr. Kumar's prior work on confidence preferences and shortcut solutions. The approach will involve: 1) Formalizing the loss landscapes and implicit gradients for objectives like reconstruction (MAE), denoising score matching (diffusion), and online clustering. 2) Analyzing how these objectives constrain the feature space, using measures of representation uniformity and tolerance to input perturbations. 3) Empirically validating the theory by pre-training medium-scale vision transformers (ViT-Small) and language models (e.g., GPT-2 architecture) on datasets like ImageNet-1k and C4, then fine-tuning on curated datasets with spurious correlations to test shortcut avoidance and confidence calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified theoretical lens for comparing non-contrastive SSL methods, predicting their downstream reliability. It will yield practical guidelines for selecting pre-training objectives based on desired model properties like calibration and robustness, moving beyond empirical benchmarking.
proposals.novelty: While existing theory is siloed within specific SSL families (e.g., contrastive), our work seeks a cross-objective theory connecting pre-training to confidence and shortcuts, a synthesis not yet attempted in the literature.

proposals.title: Theoretical and Empirical Conditions for Disentangling Domain and Class Features in Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Domain shift and spurious correlations remain major obstacles to robust generalization in machine learning. While methods like domain-invariant representation learning aim to separate domain from class features, their theoretical guarantees are often brittle, and empirical success is inconsistent. A formal analysis of the conditions for successful disentanglement is needed to guide the design of more reliable algorithms, particularly for vision-language models where multi-modal data can entangle these factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful disentanglement of domain and class features is contingent upon a specific, quantifiable relationship between dataset diversity (e.g., number of domains per class) and model architecture (e.g., bottleneck capacity), and that this can be measured via the relative confidence of a model's predictions across domains.
proposals.method: We will extend the theoretical framework of information bottleneck and invariant risk minimization to derive necessary conditions for disentanglement. Empirically, we will construct controlled synthetic datasets (e.g., colored MNIST variants) and real-world benchmarks (DomainNet, WILDS) to test these conditions. We will fine-tune vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) and standard CNNs, using the confidence preference analysis from Kumar's prior work to quantify the alignment of model confidence with true class versus spurious domain features under varying dataset compositions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable set of conditions for feature disentanglement, moving beyond heuristic approaches. It will yield practical guidelines for dataset curation and model selection to improve out-of-distribution robustness. The integration of confidence metrics offers a new diagnostic tool for evaluating representation learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally linking dataset statistics to the feasibility of disentanglement within contemporary fine-tuning paradigms and using confidence-based metrics, rather than just loss or accuracy, as a core analytical tool for diagnosing feature alignment.

proposals.title: Beyond Synthetic Shifts: Measuring and Improving Model Confidence Under Real-World Distributional Change
proposals.motivation: Current evaluations of model robustness and confidence calibration under domain shift often rely on synthetic or narrow benchmarks (e.g., ImageNet-C, MNIST variations). This fails to capture the complex, multi-faceted nature of real-world distribution shifts, where changes in style, context, and composition co-occur. Grounding evaluation in such realistic shifts is critical for deploying reliable models in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modern fine-tuned models, including generative classifiers, will exhibit systematic overconfidence and degraded performance under complex, real-world domain shifts, and that a confidence preference-based training objective can mitigate this failure.
proposals.method: We will construct a new benchmark, 'RealShift', by curating naturally occurring domain pairs from existing datasets (e.g., Clipart vs. Real Photos in DomainNet, artistic vs. photographic styles, web-crawled vs. professionally shot images). We will evaluate the confidence and accuracy of models from Kumar's prior work, including generative classifiers and models trained with confidence preferences. The core intervention will be to adapt the 'Relative Confidence Estimation via Confidence Preferences' method, using contrastive pairs sampled *across* these realistic domains during fine-tuning to explicitly shape the model's confidence landscape under shift.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A new benchmark for evaluating model confidence under realistic, complex domain shifts, moving beyond synthetic corruption. 2) Empirical evidence on the failure modes of current fine-tuning methods in these settings. 3) A demonstrated adaptation of confidence preference training that improves robustness to real-world distributional change.
proposals.novelty: While domain shift is well-studied, the targeted analysis of *confidence behavior* and the application of *confidence preference learning* to diverse, non-synthetic, real-world shifts is novel. It directly tests whether confidence-based training methods generalize beyond the controlled shifts they were developed on.

proposals.title: Metric-Fine: Enhancing Fine-Tuning with Metric Learning for Robust and Calibrated Language Models
proposals.motivation: Standard fine-tuning of large language models (LLMs) on downstream tasks often leads to overfitting, catastrophic forgetting, and poorly calibrated confidence estimates, as highlighted by pre-2026 work on shortcut learning and confidence. This fragility limits model robustness and trustworthiness in real-world applications. Integrating structured learning objectives during fine-tuning is a promising but underexplored avenue to address these issues.
proposals.hypothesis: Augmenting the standard cross-entropy loss during fine-tuning with metric learning objectives (e.g., triplet or contrastive loss) will enforce a more structured and separable latent representation, leading to improved model robustness, better out-of-distribution generalization, and more calibrated confidence estimates on the target task.
proposals.method: We propose a fine-tuning framework where the loss is a weighted sum of the task-specific cross-entropy and a metric learning loss. For a given batch, we will construct triplets (anchor, positive, negative) based on class labels or semantic similarity. We will experiment with triplet margin loss and supervised contrastive loss, applied to the final-layer embeddings of the model. The approach will be evaluated by fine-tuning pre-trained models like BERT or T5 on a suite of NLP datasets (e.g., GLUE, ANLI) and benchmarking against standard fine-tuning, measuring not only accuracy but also calibration (via ECE) and robustness to distribution shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical and theoretically grounded fine-tuning recipe that improves model generalization and calibration. It will contribute a new perspective on bridging representation learning (metric learning) with parameter-efficient adaptation, advancing beyond simple cross-entropy fine-tuning. The resulting models will be more reliable and less prone to shortcut learning.
proposals.novelty: While metric learning is established in computer vision, its systematic integration into the fine-tuning stage of modern LLMs for NLP tasks is novel. The key innovation is framing fine-tuning as a joint representation and task learning problem, explicitly shaping the embedding space to improve downstream properties like confidence calibration, which connects directly to the researcher's prior work on relative confidence estimation.

proposals.title: Confidence-Calibrated Few-Shot Source Transfer: Mitigating Overfitting in Extreme Low-Resource Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Fine-tuning large pre-trained models on small, labeled source datasets is a critical challenge for low-resource domains, but it is prone to severe overfitting and catastrophic forgetting. Prior work by the researcher on relative confidence estimation suggests that models' internal confidence signals could guide more robust adaptation. This proposal addresses the gap in understanding how to effectively leverage both the pre-trained model's inductive bias and extremely limited (few-shot) source labels for reliable transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and constraining the fine-tuning process using the model's pre-training confidence landscape will prevent overfitting to few-shot source labels, leading to better generalization and calibration on target tasks compared to standard fine-tuning or prompt-based methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Confidence Preferences' framework to the few-shot fine-tuning setting. For a given few-shot source dataset, we will fine-tune a pre-trained language model (e.g., T5 or GPT-2) with a novel regularization loss that penalizes deviations from the model's pre-training confidence distribution on in-context examples. This will be combined with a distribution matching objective (e.g., MMD) on hidden representations between pre-trained and fine-tuned models, using datasets like FLAN or specialized few-shot NLP benchmarks. We will ablate the contribution of confidence-based regularization against baseline methods like adapter tuning and LoRA.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, confidence-aware algorithm for few-shot source adaptation that is more robust to overfitting. It will yield empirical evidence on how confidence signals from pre-training can be harnessed for data-efficient learning, contributing a new perspective at the intersection of transfer learning and model calibration.
proposals.novelty: While few-shot learning and fine-tuning are well-studied, using a model's inherent confidence preferences—a signal shown to be informative of its inductive biases—as a direct regularizer during parameter updates in extreme low-data regimes is a novel and non-obvious approach.

proposals.title: Confidence Calibration in High-Dimensional Non-Vision Domains: A Study of Language and Genomic Models
proposals.motivation: Prior work on confidence estimation and shortcut learning has been predominantly validated in computer vision. As large language models (LLMs) and genomic foundation models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains like medical text analysis and variant calling, understanding and improving their confidence calibration is critical for reliability. It remains unclear if theories of relative confidence and generative robustness generalize to these high-dimensional, discrete, and structured data modalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the principles of relative confidence estimation via confidence preferences and the shortcut-avoidance of generative classifiers, established in vision, will hold but require domain-specific adaptations to effectively scale and calibrate models for text and genomic sequence data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the 'Confidence Preferences' framework to decoder-only LLMs (e.g., LLaMA-2) and genomic transformers (e.g., Nucleotide Transformer) by fine-tuning them to predict which of two perturbed inputs (e.g., via synonym substitution or k-mer shuffling) the base model is more confident on. We will concurrently train generative classifiers (e.g., GPT-2 style autoregressive models) on text classification (AG News) and genomic sequence function prediction (DeepSEA tasks) and compare their shortcut susceptibility to discriminative baselines. Evaluation will use calibration error (ECE), out-of-distribution detection (AUROC), and robustness to spurious feature ablation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic evaluation of vision-derived confidence and robustness theories in key non-vision domains, yielding new calibration techniques for LLMs and genomic models. It will produce publicly available fine-tuned checkpoints and a benchmark for confidence estimation in genomics, advancing trustworthy AI in biology and NLP.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in scaling and testing core theories from Ananya Kumar's pre-2026 vision-centric research to the under-explored, high-stakes domains of text and genomics, moving beyond the common vision-language multimodal focus to pure sequence data.

proposals.title: Dual-Confidence Classifiers: Mitigating Shortcuts via Domain-Variant Feature Partitioning and Specialized Training
proposals.motivation: Modern deep learning models often exploit spurious, domain-variant features as shortcut solutions, leading to poor generalization on out-of-distribution data. This is a critical failure mode for real-world deployment, where data domains shift. Pre-2026 work on generative classifiers and confidence estimation suggests that models can learn more robust features, but a systematic method to isolate and leverage domain-variant features for building specialized, high-confidence sub-networks is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly partitioning a model's feature space into domain-variant and domain-invariant components, we can train separate, high-confidence classifiers on the domain-variant stream that, when combined with a robust base classifier, improve overall accuracy and calibration across shifting domains.
proposals.method: We will design a dual-stream architecture where a shared backbone feeds into two branches: one trained to maximize domain prediction loss (extracting domain-variant features) and another trained with adversarial domain confusion or IRM-inspired penalties (for domain-invariant features). Each branch will have its own classifier head. Drawing from Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation, we will implement a confidence-based gating mechanism that, at inference, routes samples to the classifier (domain-variant or invariant) with higher self-estimated confidence. Training will use a multi-objective loss on datasets like DomainNet or WILDS.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel architectural framework for controllable feature disentanglement and specialized classifier training. It will provide new empirical evidence on how confidence estimation can guide inference-time model selection, advancing methods for out-of-distribution generalization beyond standard fine-tuning or domain adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard domain adaptation that seeks to discard domain-variant signals, this proposal intentionally cultivates them into a high-confidence specialist, creating a complementary dual-classifier system. The integration of explicit feature partitioning with confidence-based routing is a new synthesis of disentanglement and confidence estimation techniques.

proposals.title: Scaffolded Robustness: Architectural Coupling of Narrow Experts with Broad-Spectrum Monitors for OOD Safety
proposals.motivation: Foundation models trained on narrow, high-quality domains achieve peak in-distribution performance but suffer catastrophic failures on out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs, limiting their safe deployment. Current robustness methods like fine-tuning on broader data often degrade the expert's specialized capability. There is a critical need for an architectural paradigm that preserves narrow-expert performance while providing systematic OOD robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: A foundation model's OOD fragility can be mitigated by architecturally coupling a narrowly-trained primary expert with a smaller, broadly-trained 'Robustness Scaffold' that monitors for distributional shift, and that this scaffold can effectively guide the expert to safer behavior without compromising its core in-domain accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-component system: 1) A large 'Expert' model fine-tuned on a narrow, high-quality dataset (e.g., medical text, synthetic code). 2) A parallel 'Robustness Scaffold' model, a smaller transformer or efficient net, trained on diverse, noisy data (e.g., a mix of C4, Reddit, adversarial examples). The scaffold will continuously compute a relative confidence score, inspired by Kumar's prior work on confidence preferences, to detect OOD inputs. Upon detection, it will either inject generalized feature priors into the expert's intermediate layers via cross-attention or trigger a fallback to a safe, predefined 'constitutional' response mode.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel architectural blueprint for building high-performance, fail-safe foundation models. It will deliver a concrete method for OOD detection and intervention that preserves in-domain expertise, advancing towards more reliable and trustworthy AI systems. We will empirically validate the approach on language and vision benchmarks, measuring in-domain performance retention versus robustness gains.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard ensemble or multi-task learning, this proposal enforces a strict architectural separation of concerns and uses the broad-scope model solely as a monitor and guide, not a primary predictor. The novel intervention mechanism—using confidence-based triggers to inject priors or enact safe modes—moves beyond simple weighting or switching.

proposals.title: Targeted Immunization for Foundational Skills: Training Language Models on High-Fidelity Synthetic Data for Robust Reasoning
proposals.motivation: Current foundation models are trained on vast, noisy internet corpora, which embed societal biases, logical inconsistencies, and shortcut solutions. This leads to unreliable reasoning, poor calibration, and brittleness in specialized domains like mathematics and code. A new paradigm is needed to build models with robust, foundational skills by design, analogous to how mRNA vaccines provide a precise blueprint for immunity.
proposals.hypothesis: A language model trained exclusively on a narrow, high-fidelity synthetic dataset that perfectly encapsulates the logical structures and reasoning patterns of a target domain (e.g., formal mathematics) will exhibit more robust, generalizable, and confident foundational skills in that domain than a model trained on broad, noisy natural data, even after fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will generate a synthetic corpus for a domain (e.g., mathematical proofs) using formal grammars and rule-based engines to ensure logical perfection. We will pre-train a transformer-based language model (e.g., a decoder-only architecture like GPT-2/3 scale) from scratch on this corpus. Its performance and confidence will be rigorously evaluated against a baseline model pre-trained on natural data (e.g., The Pile) and then fine-tuned on the same domain. We will employ Ananya Kumar's recent methods for relative confidence estimation and analysis of shortcut solutions to measure robustness and generalization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a new 'targeted immunization' training paradigm, demonstrating that high-fidelity synthetic data can yield superior foundational reasoning. It will contribute a framework for building reliable, specialized foundation models and offer new insights into how data quality and structure shape model capabilities and confidence.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the dominant scaling hypothesis by advocating for extreme data quality and specificity over breadth for building foundational skills. It directly connects the vaccine development analogy to AI training, proposing synthetic data as a precise 'blueprint' rather than a broad, attenuated stimulus.

proposals.title: Narrow-Foundation Models: Calibration, Confidence, and Generalization from Constrained Data Regimes
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in foundation models is training on massive, broad, and often noisy web-scale datasets. This approach, while powerful, introduces well-documented issues like shortcut learning, memorization of biases, and poor calibration on out-of-distribution tasks. Investigating models trained on intentionally narrow, high-quality, or synthetic data domains provides a crucial counterpoint to understand the necessity of scale versus the benefits of data curation and purity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that foundation models trained on narrow, high-quality domains will exhibit superior calibration and confidence estimation on in-domain tasks and more robust, compositional generalization by avoiding spurious correlations, compared to broadly-trained models of similar parameter count, at the cost of reduced breadth of knowledge.
proposals.method: We will train a series of decoder-only language models (e.g., GPT-2/NeoX architecture) from scratch on curated datasets: 1) a narrow, high-quality corpus (e.g., peer-reviewed STEM abstracts), 2) a synthetically generated domain (e.g., code from a DSL), and 3) a mixture of both. We will benchmark these against a broadly-trained baseline (e.g., a Pile-trained model). Evaluation will leverage Ananya Kumar's recent work on relative confidence estimation via confidence preferences to measure calibration. We will also assess generalization via systematic out-of-domain probes and fine-tuning efficiency on downstream tasks within the narrow domain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic empirical framework for evaluating the trade-offs of narrow versus broad pre-training. It will yield new insights into how data distribution properties affect model confidence and generalization, potentially informing more efficient and trustworthy model development pipelines. The findings could validate a 'quality-over-quantity' approach for specialized AI applications.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the core assumption of modern foundation model scaling by rigorously testing the limits and benefits of narrow, high-integrity data. It uniquely combines this training paradigm with advanced confidence estimation techniques to quantitatively measure the hypothesized improvements in model reliability and robustness.

proposals.title: Ensemble Specialization via Controlled Divergence: Training Complementary Foundation Models on a Single Curated Corpus
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in foundation model development is scaling single models on ever-larger datasets, raising significant ethical, legal, and environmental concerns. This proposal addresses a critical alternative: can we use compute not for data scaling, but to create a more robust and capable system from a single, high-quality, and smaller dataset? This directly tackles issues of model brittleness, single-point failure, and the unsustainable data acquisition practices prevalent pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: By architecting a joint training process with diversity-inducing objectives and controlled parameter sharing, a coordinated ensemble of smaller models trained on the same curated corpus will develop fundamentally different, complementary internal representations, leading to superior collective robustness and capability compared to a single model of equivalent total compute.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-objective optimization framework for training an ensemble of transformer-based language models. Each model will share a common base architecture but will be specialized via: (1) a novel 'confidence-weighted disagreement loss' that encourages divergent predictions on ambiguous data points, building on Kumar's prior work on relative confidence; (2) a controlled, sparse parameter sharing mechanism (e.g., cross-attention adapters) to facilitate communication while preserving specialization; and (3) a fine-tuning stage using task-specific datasets to finalize complementary roles (e.g., factual recall vs. creative generation). The training corpus will be a single, meticulously filtered dataset like the Pile or a custom legal/medical collection.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel training framework for efficient, specialized ensembles, a concrete methodology for inducing complementary world models from a single data source, and empirical evidence that such ensembles outperform monolithic models in out-of-distribution robustness and task coverage. It provides a practical path toward capable AI systems that mitigate data-scale externalities.
proposals.novelty: While ensembles and multi-task learning are established, the core novelty is using intensive compute specifically to *force divergence* in foundational representations from a *single* data stream, moving beyond simple averaging of independently trained models or multi-modal fusion. This inverts the standard scaling hypothesis by focusing compute on specialization rather than aggregation.

proposals.title: EcoCLIP: Ecological Niche Modeling for Robust and Diverse Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Modern large-scale pre-training often yields brittle models that rely on spurious correlations and fail under distribution shifts. Drawing on pre-2026 work in out-of-distribution generalization and multi-domain learning, we argue that current contrastive objectives like InfoNCE optimize for a single, monolithic representation, lacking the functional diversity needed for systemic resilience. This proposal addresses the need to engineer AI systems that are not just high-performing but inherently robust and adaptable, akin to healthy ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling data domains as ecological niches within a contrastive learning framework and applying principles of niche differentiation and functional redundancy, we can train foundation models that develop a more diverse and resilient set of feature representations, leading to superior robustness to domain shifts and adversarial perturbations.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel contrastive loss that extends frameworks like SimCLR or CLIP. The core innovation is a structured similarity matrix where positive pairs are defined within a 'niche' (e.g., a specific data domain or augmentation family), and negative pairs are sampled strategically across niches to enforce diversity. We will integrate domain-adversarial constraints from pre-2026 methods like DANN to ensure niche-invariant core features while promoting niche-specific specializations. Training will involve multi-domain datasets (e.g., a union of ImageNet, DomainNet, and conceptual captions) to simulate a complex ecosystem.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a new pre-training paradigm grounded in ecological theory, 2) the EcoCLIP objective and training protocol for cultivating model diversity, and 3) empirical evidence linking representational diversity metrics (inspired by Kumar's work on confidence and distribution) to measurable gains in robustness on benchmarks like ImageNet-C and WILDS.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task and domain-invariant learning exist, explicitly using ecological resilience as a guiding principle for the contrastive objective itself is novel. The approach moves beyond minimizing a single loss to actively managing the 'population dynamics' of features within a model, a non-obvious synthesis of contrastive learning and ecological modeling.

proposals.title: Adversarial Data Distillation: A Compute-Driven Loop for Synthesizing Minimal, Robust Training Sets
proposals.motivation: Training large models on massive datasets is computationally prohibitive, while training on small, static datasets often leads to brittle models with poor generalization and shortcut learning. Recent work in dataset distillation and confidence estimation provides tools, but lacks a systematic, automated method to create a minimal dataset that guarantees robustness against a vast distribution of potential inputs. This research addresses the core tension between data efficiency and model robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: A compute-intensive, adversarial loop that iteratively trains a model on a candidate distilled set, uses generative models to synthesize data targeting its low-confidence regions, and validates against a large background corpus can automatically produce a small dataset that yields models with robustness comparable to those trained on orders-of-magnitude more data.
proposals.method: We propose an iterative algorithm: 1) Initialize a small distilled dataset. 2) Fine-tune a target model on it. 3) Use a large language model (LLM) or diffusion model, conditioned on the target model's confidence scores (inspired by Kumar's confidence preference work), to generate synthetic adversarial examples that probe low-confidence regions. 4) Adversarially filter these candidates by evaluating their impact on a 'validation' model trained on the large background corpus. 5) Selectively incorporate the most informative failures into the distilled set. The loop uses compute to replace human curation with automated, distribution-aware probing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for automated dataset creation that directly optimizes for robustness and coverage. It will yield a method to produce ultra-efficient, high-performance training sets and offer new insights into the relationship between data diversity, model confidence, and generalization, moving beyond static distillation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop, adversarial use of massive compute to dynamically synthesize and curate data based on a model's evolving weaknesses, rather than distilling a fixed dataset once. This integrates generative synthesis, confidence-based probing, and adversarial validation in a unified process for automated dataset optimization.

proposals.title: SourceTracker for AI: Deconvolving Training Data Populations from Model Representations
proposals.motivation: As foundation models are trained on massive, often undisclosed, multi-source datasets, auditing their data composition is critical for transparency, fairness, and safety. Current membership inference and data extraction attacks are limited to recovering individual points, lacking tools to characterize the broader statistical makeup of the training corpus. This creates a need for principled methods to audit the proportional influence of different data populations within a model.
proposals.hypothesis: The internal representations and output distributions of a foundation model contain blended statistical signatures of its constituent training datasets, analogous to a mixed DNA sample in metagenomics. These signatures can be deconvolved using adapted reference-based algorithms to recover the composition and relative proportions of the original training data populations.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Bayesian source-tracking framework of tools like SourceTracker. First, we will define 'reference profiles' by extracting feature co-occurrence statistics (e.g., from intermediate layer activations or attention patterns) from known, held-out datasets. Then, we will treat the deployed model's representations on a strategic query set as a 'mixed sample'. By solving an optimization problem that minimizes the divergence between the mixed sample's statistics and a linear combination of the reference profiles, we will infer the mixture proportions. The method will be validated on controlled language models fine-tuned on known mixtures of distinct corpora (e.g., arXiv, GitHub, Books).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel auditing tool for model transparency, enabling practitioners to estimate the influence of different data sources post-hoc. It will advance the field of data provenance by moving beyond point-wise attacks to population-level characterization, offering a new lens for analyzing dataset biases and license compliance in foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work extracts discrete training examples, this approach is the first to formally frame training data auditing as a metagenomic-style deconvolution problem, aiming to recover statistical populations rather than individual instances. It uniquely leverages the researcher's expertise in confidence estimation and model analysis to define the 'genetic markers' for data sources.

proposals.title: Auditing Foundation Models via Confidence-Guided Training Data Extraction
proposals.motivation: As foundation models are deployed widely, auditing their training data composition is critical for verifying licensing, detecting memorization of private data, and diagnosing biases. Existing membership inference attacks are limited to binary classification, while full data extraction methods are computationally prohibitive and lack precision for large models. A method to efficiently recover representative, high-fidelity data samples is needed for practical model auditing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's internal confidence distribution, particularly its relative confidence preferences, can be leveraged as a signal to guide gradient-based optimization for extracting high-likelihood training samples, enabling more efficient and representative data recovery than prior approaches.
proposals.method: We will develop a method that fine-tunes a 'probe' generative model (e.g., a diffusion model) using gradients from the target frozen foundation model. The key innovation is using the model's own confidence preferences—contrasting high vs. low confidence outputs for candidate data—as a training signal for the probe. We will adapt techniques from Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation to design a loss function that maximizes the divergence between the target model's confidence on extracted data and a reference distribution, iteratively steering the probe to generate samples that are highly representative of the original training distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, confidence-based framework for training data extraction that is more sample-efficient and scalable than brute-force gradient ascent on pixels/tokens. It will provide a new auditing tool for model developers and regulators. The method also offers insights into the relationship between a model's confidence landscape and its memorization patterns.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in using a model's own confidence preferences as the primary guide for data extraction, moving beyond simple loss or gradient magnitude signals. It uniquely combines fine-tuning a generative probe with a confidence-based objective derived from the auditor's prior research themes.

proposals.title: Gradual Domain Adaptation with Data-Constrained Intermediates: Theory and Confidence-Guided Sampling
proposals.motivation: Gradual Domain Adaptation (GDA) theory assumes access to infinite unlabeled data at each intermediate domain step, enabling smooth adaptation. This is unrealistic for many real-world sequential learning problems, such as adapting a model across a series of user groups or geographic locations, where data collection is costly and per-step data is limited. Relaxing this assumption is crucial for making GDA practically applicable.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling data scarcity at intermediate steps and using model confidence to guide selective data usage and generation, we can develop a theoretically sound and empirically effective GDA framework that outperforms standard methods under realistic data constraints.
proposals.method: We will develop a theoretical framework for GDA with bounded intermediate data, analyzing error propagation and deriving sampling strategies. Algorithmically, we will extend Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation to create a 'confidence-guided replay' mechanism, prioritizing high-confidence pseudo-labeled examples from previous domains for fine-tuning when current-domain data is scarce. We will also explore controlled data augmentation using pre-trained generative models (e.g., diffusion models) to synthesize plausible intermediate samples, constrained by confidence and distributional metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive theory for data-constrained GDA, a practical algorithm leveraging confidence for robust adaptation, and a new benchmark for evaluating GDA under limited data. It will bridge the gap between theoretical GDA and its deployment in resource-limited settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal relaxation of the infinite-data assumption in GDA theory and the integration of confidence-based active learning principles—rather than passive sampling—to strategically manage limited data across a sequential adaptation path.

proposals.title: Robust Self-Training Under Abrupt Distribution Shifts via Confidence-Guided Curriculum and Forgetting
proposals.motivation: Self-training methods for continual learning typically assume gradual data drift, but real-world deployments (e.g., vision-based monitoring, evolving language use) face sudden, large distributional shifts. Pre-2026 methods like pseudo-labeling and fine-tuning can catastrophically fail in such scenarios, as they reinforce outdated model biases, leading to irreversible error propagation and performance collapse.
proposals.hypothesis: A self-training framework that dynamically estimates model confidence to detect abrupt shifts and employs a curriculum of selective forgetting and replay can recover model performance without requiring explicit shift labels or a separate validation set.
proposals.method: We will extend Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation to create a shift detector monitoring the distribution of confidence scores on pseudo-labels. Upon detecting an abrupt drop, the framework will: 1) freeze the current model and initiate a new 'student' model, 2) implement a confidence-guided curriculum where high-confidence new examples and a replay buffer of high-confidence old examples are used for training, and 3) incorporate a selective forgetting mechanism, inspired by generative classifier insights, to actively downweight parameters associated with shortcut features from the old domain. We will test on constructed streams from datasets like DomainNet and evolving text corpora.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical self-training algorithm resilient to non-gradual distribution shifts, expanding the applicability of continual learning to more realistic settings. It will contribute a theoretical and empirical analysis of confidence dynamics as a shift signal and the role of controlled forgetting in recovery.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior continual learning focusing on gradual drift or rehearsal buffers, this explicitly targets recovery from unlabeled, abrupt shifts by combining confidence-based shift detection with a training curriculum that balances replay and intentional forgetting, a direction less explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: PathFinder: Gradual Adaptation from Multiple Source Domains via Confidence-Guided Curriculum
proposals.motivation: Domain adaptation research has predominantly focused on a single source-target pair, a simplification that fails to capture real-world scenarios where relevant knowledge is distributed across multiple, heterogeneous sources (e.g., multiple datasets, modalities, or task formulations). Directly aggregating these sources can lead to conflicting signals and shortcut learning, hindering robust adaptation to a novel target domain. This work addresses the under-explored problem of multi-source domain adaptation (MSDA) by explicitly modeling the *path* of adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constructing a gradual, curriculum-based adaptation path from multiple source domains to a target, where the sequence is determined by the model's own relative confidence on intermediate tasks, will yield more robust and generalizable representations than direct or uniformly weighted multi-source adaptation.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will use the principles from 'Relative Confidence Estimation via Confidence Preferences' to construct a directed graph of domains, where edges represent high-confidence transferability. Second, we will train a model (e.g., a vision-language model fine-tuned on a sequence of tasks) along the highest-confidence path using a progressive fine-tuning schedule, regularized to avoid catastrophic forgetting and shortcut solutions as in 'Generative Classifiers Avoid Shortcut Solutions'. We will validate on multi-source benchmarks created from datasets like DomainNet and diverse text corpora.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, confidence-driven methodology for MSDA that moves beyond static source combination. It will yield insights into how learning trajectories affect generalization and produce a practical algorithm for settings where data arrives from multiple, disparate distributions. The work will also extend confidence estimation techniques from single-model calibration to inter-domain relationships.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty lies in inverting the standard MSDA assumption of a fixed source mixture by dynamically constructing an optimal adaptation *path*, ordered by the model's own confidence preferences, rather than treating all sources as equally accessible from the start.

proposals.title: Confidence-Guided Curriculum Sampling for Self-Training on Gradual Domain Shifts
proposals.motivation: Self-training is a powerful paradigm for adapting models to evolving data streams, but its success hinges on selecting high-quality pseudo-labels for the unlabeled target data. In gradual domain shift scenarios, where the data distribution evolves smoothly over time, naive self-training can propagate errors or fail to capture intermediate domain structure. An optimal sampling strategy is needed to decide which data points to pseudo-label and how to weight them during training to ensure stable and efficient adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum sampling strategy, which prioritizes pseudo-labeling data points based on a model's relative confidence across adjacent domains in the stream, will yield more robust and sample-efficient self-training than standard confidence-thresholding or random sampling.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic sampling algorithm that leverages the principles from relative confidence estimation (Kumar et al., pre-2026). For each candidate batch in the unlabeled stream, we will compute a 'confidence preference' score by comparing the model's confidence on the sample to its confidence on a held-out reference set from the most recent adapted domain. Samples with high relative confidence will be prioritized for pseudo-labeling. We will integrate this with a weighting mechanism that adjusts the loss contribution of each pseudo-labeled sample based on its estimated reliability, preventing overfitting to noisy labels. The algorithm will be evaluated on established gradual shift benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10C, DomainNet sequences) using fine-tuned vision and language models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, confidence-aware framework for data selection in continual self-training. It will advance the field of domain adaptation by offering a practical algorithm for gradual shifts and contribute new insights into how model confidence dynamics can guide curriculum learning in non-stationary environments.
proposals.novelty: While confidence is commonly used for pseudo-label selection, our approach is novel in using *relative* confidence comparisons across temporally adjacent domains to construct an optimal curriculum, directly building on Kumar's recent pre-2026 work on confidence preferences, rather than relying on absolute thresholds.

proposals.title: Capacity-Dependent Generalization Bounds for Gradual Self-Training in Non-Stationary Environments
proposals.motivation: Gradual self-training, where a model continuously adapts to shifting data distributions using its own predictions, is crucial for real-world deployment of language and vision models. However, pre-2026 theoretical understanding of this process is limited, particularly regarding how model architecture (e.g., parameter count, layers) influences stability and error accumulation over time. A rigorous analysis is needed to guide architecture selection for reliable long-term adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for gradual self-training, there exists a critical model capacity threshold: below it, error compounds due to underfitting, while above it, excessive capacity leads to overfitting to noisy pseudo-labels, degrading long-term generalization.
proposals.method: We will derive PAC-Bayesian or Rademacher complexity-based generalization bounds for a gradual self-training process, explicitly parameterizing the bounds by model capacity (e.g., width/depth of a Transformer or CNN). The analysis will track error propagation across adaptation rounds, incorporating noise models for pseudo-labels inspired by Kumar's work on confidence estimation. We will validate the theory with controlled experiments on synthetic distribution shifts and standard benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10-C, WILDS), scaling model size (from small ResNets/Transformers to large ones) while monitoring error trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first theoretical framework linking neural architecture scale to non-stationary learning guarantees, offering concrete principles for capacity selection in continual learning. It will also yield practical insights for stabilizing self-training in applications like autonomous systems and language model fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes static self-training or domain adaptation, our focus on capacity-dependent *gradual* adaptation dynamics, integrating theoretical error bounds with architectural scaling, is novel. It directly connects the researcher's expertise in confidence estimation with foundational learning theory.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Gradual Domain Adaptation via Self-Training with Confidence-Guided Pseudo-Labeling
proposals.motivation: Real-world deployment of models often involves gradual, non-stationary shifts in data distribution, such as evolving user language or changing visual environments. Standard continual or domain adaptation methods typically assume access to large batches of new data or labeled samples, which is impractical for many edge or privacy-sensitive applications. This work addresses the critical, under-explored scenario where a model must adapt online using only a small stream of unlabeled data at each time step.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a self-training framework, which leverages a model's own confidence estimates to select and refine high-quality pseudo-labels from sparse unlabeled data, can enable stable and effective few-shot gradual adaptation without catastrophic forgetting or error accumulation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the confidence estimation techniques from Kumar's prior work (e.g., 'Language Models Prefer What They Know') to a continual self-training loop. At each adaptation step, for each new few-shot batch, the model will generate predictions and associated confidence scores. Only samples with confidence exceeding a dynamically adjusted threshold will be used to create pseudo-labels for a subsequent fine-tuning step. We will integrate a rehearsal buffer of high-confidence pseudo-labeled examples from previous steps, regularized using techniques inspired by 'Generative Classifiers Avoid Shortcut Solutions' to prevent shortcut learning on the small data stream. Evaluation will use established gradual domain adaptation benchmarks (e.g., shifting CIFAR-10-C, or a temporal language dataset) under a strict few-shot unlabeled per-step protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical algorithm for low-data continual adaptation, expanding the applicability of domain adaptation to real-time, data-scarce scenarios. It will contribute new insights into the interplay between model confidence, pseudo-label quality, and stability in online learning, and release a benchmark for evaluating few-shot gradual adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While self-training and confidence estimation exist, their combination for the specific constraint of per-step few-shot unlabeled data in a gradual adaptation setting is novel. The core innovation is the confidence-guided control mechanism for stabilizing the feedback loop in an extremely low-data regime, preventing the rapid degradation seen in standard methods.

proposals.title: Gradual Domain Adaptation for Generative Models via Confidence-Guided Curriculum Learning
proposals.motivation: Adapting generative models like GANs across domains is crucial for applications like data augmentation and style transfer, but direct fine-tuning on a distant target domain often leads to catastrophic forgetting or mode collapse. Pre-2026 work on gradual adaptation for discriminative models shows promise, but its application to generative models, which must maintain sample diversity and quality, remains underexplored. This gap limits the robust deployment of generative models in real-world scenarios with shifting data distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a curriculum of intermediate domains, guided by a model's own relative confidence estimates, will enable more stable and higher-quality adaptation of generative adversarial networks (GANs) compared to direct fine-tuning or existing unsupervised domain adaptation methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the gradual shift framework to StyleGAN2. A sequence of interpolated domains will be constructed between source and target datasets (e.g., FFHQ to MetFaces). Drawing on Kumar's work on relative confidence, we will use the discriminator's confidence preferences to automatically schedule the curriculum, progressing only when the model is confidently generating samples for the current domain. Adaptation will proceed via iterative fine-tuning on this confidence-guided path, with regularization to preserve the source model's latent structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for stable generative model adaptation, reducing mode collapse. It will extend the theory of gradual adaptation from discriminative to generative tasks. The resulting framework will enable practical cross-domain generation where large, direct target datasets are unavailable.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the fusion of a gradual adaptation curriculum with generative model fine-tuning, using the model's internal confidence signals—a concept proven for classifiers—to guide the generative adaptation process itself, which is a non-trivial dual problem.

proposals.title: Confidence-Weighted Federated Adaptation: Mitigating Distribution Drift in On-Device Language Models
proposals.motivation: Federated learning (FL) enables on-device model personalization, but real-world local data distributions drift over time, degrading performance. Standard FL aggregation (e.g., FedAvg) treats all client updates equally, which is suboptimal when data quality and relevance vary. Pre-2026, methods to handle non-IID and drifting data in FL remain nascent, lacking principled ways to leverage model self-assessment for robust aggregation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that weighting client updates in federated aggregation by the model's local confidence on its own adapted data—inspired by relative confidence estimation—will yield a global model more robust to distribution drift compared to standard averaging.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Confidence Preferences' framework to FL. Each client fine-tunes a base language model (e.g., a compressed T5 or BERT variant) on its local streaming data. Instead of sending only parameter deltas, clients will also compute a confidence score for their update, derived from the model's self-evaluation on a held-out validation set drawn from the recent data distribution. The server will aggregate updates using a weighted average, where weights are a function of these confidence signals, dynamically prioritizing more reliable adaptations. We will test this on decentralized text datasets simulating temporal concept drift.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, confidence-aware aggregation algorithm for federated learning that is explicitly designed for drifting distributions. It will provide empirical evidence on how model self-assessment can improve robustness in decentralized settings and offer a practical method for sustainable on-device adaptation without catastrophic forgetting of global knowledge.
proposals.novelty: While confidence estimation exists and FL methods for non-IID data are studied, the integration of explicit, communication-efficient confidence signals to weight updates against temporal drift in on-device FL is a novel synthesis of Ananya Kumar's prior themes.

proposals.title: Confidence-Guided Self-Training for Domain Adaptation with Scarce Source Labels
proposals.motivation: Domain adaptation research has focused heavily on scenarios with abundant labeled source data and unlabeled target data. However, in many real-world applications, such as adapting a model to a new sensor or a niche domain, collecting substantial labeled source data is also costly and impractical. Pre-2026 methods often fail when source labels are extremely limited, as they cannot reliably estimate the source distribution or the domain shift.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's internal confidence on a small set of labeled source examples can be used to guide the generation and selection of pseudo-labels on the unlabeled target data, creating a self-reinforcing training loop that is robust to the initial scarcity of supervision.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we will fine-tune a pre-trained model (e.g., a vision transformer or a large language model) on the few labeled source examples. Second, we will employ a self-training loop where the model generates pseudo-labels for the abundant unlabeled target data.
