Crucially, we will adapt Ananya Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation to create a 'confidence preference' score, which will be used to weight and filter pseudo-labels, prioritizing high-confidence, high-agreement target samples for the next training iteration. This prevents error accumulation from noisy pseudo-labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for domain adaptation under extreme label scarcity in the source domain, a largely underexplored setting. It will demonstrate how model confidence, rather than just prediction outputs, can stabilize self-training. We will validate our approach on established domain adaptation benchmarks (e.g., Office-Home, DomainNet) under a severely limited source label protocol.
proposals.novelty: While self-training is known, its application guided by principled confidence estimation from a severely under-trained source model is novel. This bridges Kumar's work on confidence with the practical constraint of source data scarcity, moving beyond standard unsupervised domain adaptation assumptions.

proposals.title: Reverse Domain Adaptation: Fine-Tuning from Labeled Target to Unlabeled Source for Robust Confidence Calibration
proposals.motivation: Standard unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) assumes a labeled source and unlabeled target, but real-world deployment often involves a model trained on a clean, curated target-like dataset that must then generalize to a more diverse, unlabeled source-like distribution (e.g., a model trained on high-quality web data applied to noisy user uploads). This reverse adaptation setting is underexplored. Pre-2026, the field lacks methods to leverage a small, high-quality labeled target set to adapt to a broader, unlabeled source, a critical step for robust real-world AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fine-tuning a model pre-trained on a labeled target-like distribution using a confidence-based objective on unlabeled source-like data will yield better generalization and calibration on the true source distribution than standard UDA or direct target training.
proposals.method: We will adapt a pre-trained language model (e.g., T5 or a decoder-only model) fine-tuned on a labeled 'target' dataset. The core algorithm involves a two-stage process: 1) Train a relative confidence estimator (inspired by Kumar's prior work) on the target data to predict which of two model outputs is more reliable. 2) Use this estimator to guide self-training on the unlabeled source data, prioritizing examples where the model's predictions align with high relative confidence. We will benchmark against standard UDA baselines (e.g., DANN) and consistency-training methods on NLP datasets with controlled distribution shifts (e.g., sentiment analysis across domains).
proposals.contribution: This work will formalize the 'reverse adaptation' problem setting and provide a novel, confidence-driven fine-tuning algorithm for it. It will contribute new insights into how models can leverage high-quality labeled data to adapt to broader, noisier distributions, with empirical results on model calibration and out-of-distribution robustness.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the canonical UDA assumption and proposing a confidence-preference framework, rather than adversarial or divergence-minimization methods, to drive adaptation from a labeled target to an unlabeled source.

proposals.title: Benchmarking and Improving Self-Supervised Learning for Distribution Shifts: A Study on WILDS 2.0 Unlabeled Data
proposals.motivation: Self-supervised learning (SSL) is a cornerstone of modern machine learning, but its robustness to real-world distribution shifts remains under-explored. The WILDS 2.0 benchmark provides a critical testbed with diverse, real-world unlabeled datasets across domains like satellite imagery and histology, where labeled data is scarce and shifts are prevalent. A systematic evaluation of SSL methods on these datasets is essential to understand their limitations and guide the development of more reliable models for deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current state-of-the-art self-supervised methods (e.g., DINO, iBOT, MAE) will exhibit significant performance degradation under the natural distribution shifts in WILDS 2.0, and that their learned representations can be improved by fine-tuning strategies that explicitly account for confidence and distributional uncertainty.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a comprehensive benchmark of pre-trained SSL models (DINOv2, iBOT, MAE) on all relevant WILDS 2.0 unlabeled datasets (e.g., FMoW-WILDS, Camelyon17-WILDS). We will then fine-tune these models using a limited set of labeled in-distribution data, evaluating their out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. Building on Kumar's prior work on confidence estimation, we will develop a novel fine-tuning objective that incorporates a relative confidence penalty to discourage overconfidence on OOD unlabeled data, aiming to produce more calibrated and robust features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale benchmark of SSL methods on the challenging, real-world shifts of WILDS 2.0, establishing new baselines for the community. It will yield actionable insights into which SSL pre-training strategies generalize best across domains and propose a novel fine-tuning method to improve OOD robustness by leveraging confidence signals.
proposals.novelty: While SSL has been benchmarked on curated shifts, a systematic study on the realistic, multifaceted shifts in WILDS 2.0 is novel. Furthermore, integrating confidence-based regularization, inspired by relative confidence estimation, into the fine-tuning of SSL models for OOD robustness is an unexplored direction.

proposals.title: Beyond Text and Vision: A Multi-Modal Benchmark for Evaluating Model Confidence and Distributional Robustness
proposals.motivation: Current benchmarks for evaluating model confidence and shortcut learning, such as those used in Kumar's prior work, are largely confined to text and static image tasks. Real-world AI systems increasingly integrate diverse data types (e.g., audio, video, sensor data) and complex, multi-step tasks. The lack of a comprehensive benchmark spanning these modalities limits our understanding of whether findings on confidence and generalization are fundamental or artifacts of narrow evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the failure modes of modern generative and discriminative models—specifically, miscalibrated confidence and susceptibility to distributional shortcuts—are exacerbated in multi-modal, real-world tasks, and that these weaknesses can be systematically measured and mitigated through targeted fine-tuning on a curated benchmark.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing benchmark framework by integrating three new datasets: 1) Audio-Visual Event Classification (using AudioSet or a similar corpus) to test cross-modal confidence alignment, 2) Robotic Manipulation Instruction Following (based on pre-2026 simulation datasets like MetaWorld) to evaluate compositional task understanding, and 3) Temporal Medical Data (e.g., MIMIC-III time-series) for structured, non-i.i.d. reasoning. We will adapt the 'Confidence Preferences' method for relative confidence estimation across modalities and employ the 'Generative Classifiers' approach to probe for shortcut learning in these new data regimes, using modality-specific encoders and a shared fusion/decision head.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first standardized benchmark for evaluating confidence calibration and distributional robustness across a spectrum of real-world tasks and data types. It will yield new insights into the transferability of confidence estimation techniques and the universality of shortcut learning, directly informing the design of more reliable, multi-modal AI systems.
proposals.novelty: While multi-modal benchmarks exist (e.g., GLUE, VQA), none are specifically designed to probe model confidence and shortcut learning behaviors across such a diverse set of non-traditional modalities and sequential decision-making tasks, creating a novel diagnostic tool for the community.

proposals.title: Confidence-Calibrated Fine-Tuning for Robust Detection and Regression in WILDS
proposals.motivation: Models for object detection and regression often fail under distribution shift, a critical challenge in real-world applications like medical imaging and autonomous systems. The WILDS benchmark highlights this adaptation gap, where standard fine-tuning leads to overconfidence on spurious correlations and poor generalization. Developing methods specifically for these structured-output tasks, which differ from classification, is an underexplored area crucial for reliable deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling and regularizing a model's confidence during fine-tuning—particularly its relative confidence across domains—will yield more robust and calibrated detectors and regressors under distribution shift in WILDS.
proposals.method: We will extend the 'Confidence Preferences' framework from Kumar's prior work to detection (e.g., Faster R-CNN) and regression heads. The method involves training a lightweight confidence estimator to predict if a sample is from the source or target domain based on the model's internal representations. This estimator's output will be used as a regularization signal during fine-tuning, discouraging overconfident predictions on shortcut features. We will specifically adapt this for bounding box and continuous value prediction, evaluating on WILDS-v2 datasets like Camelyon17 (detection) and PovertyMap (regression).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel fine-tuning algorithm tailored for detection/regression under distribution shift, advancing beyond classification-focused adaptation methods. It will deliver new state-of-the-art baselines on relevant WILDS tasks and offer insights into the relationship between model confidence, shortcut learning, and generalization in structured prediction.
proposals.novelty: While confidence estimation has been studied for classification, its application as a direct fine-tuning signal for detection and regression tasks in domain adaptation is novel. The approach uniquely combines relative confidence estimation with task-specific architectures to mitigate shortcuts, moving beyond standard adversarial or augmentation-based domain-invariant methods.

proposals.title: Confidence-Guided Test-Time Training for Language Model Adaptation Under Distribution Shift
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) degrade significantly under distribution shift, as their static parameters cannot adapt to novel inference-time data. Pre-2026 robustness literature has shown test-time training (TTT) to be effective for vision models, but its application to LLMs remains underexplored. This gap is critical because LLMs are increasingly deployed in dynamic, real-world environments where data distributions are non-stationary.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adapting LLM parameters at inference time using a self-supervised objective, guided by the model's own confidence estimates, will significantly improve performance on shifted distributions without requiring labeled data.
proposals.method: We will adapt the TTT framework from Sun et al. (2020) to autoregressive language models. For each unlabeled test batch, we will perform a few gradient steps on a self-supervised objective (e.g., masked language modeling or next-sentence prediction). Crucially, we will integrate Ananya Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation to weight the adaptation loss, focusing updates on low-confidence, high-uncertainty tokens. We will evaluate on established NLP distribution shift benchmarks (e.g., ANLI, BREAKS, or domain-shifted versions of GLUE) using models like T5 or GPT-2.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, practical method for improving LLM robustness without costly re-training or fine-tuning. It will extend the theoretical understanding of test-time adaptation from vision to language, specifically exploring the role of confidence calibration in guiding the adaptation process.
proposals.novelty: While TTT exists for vision, its application to LLMs is novel. The key innovation is the integration of a confidence-based weighting mechanism within the TTT loop, creating a closed-feedback system where the model's uncertainty directly informs its own adaptation.

proposals.title: Meta-Confidence: Unsupervised Fast Adaptation via Meta-Learned Confidence Preferences
proposals.motivation: Pre-trained models struggle to adapt to new, unlabeled target domains efficiently, a common hurdle in real-world deployment. Existing few-shot adaptation methods often require labeled data, while unsupervised domain adaptation can be slow and data-hungry. A method for rapid adaptation using only small amounts of unlabeled target data would significantly improve the practicality of foundation models.
proposals.hypothesis: A meta-learning framework, trained across diverse source tasks, can learn to adapt a model's parameters by optimizing a meta-objective based on its internal confidence distribution, enabling effective fast adaptation to new unlabeled target domains.
proposals.method: We will extend the Reptile meta-learning algorithm to an unsupervised setting. The inner loop will perform adaptation on a source task using a self-supervised loss (e.g., masked language modeling for text, rotation prediction for vision). The key innovation is the meta-objective: the outer loop will update the model's initialization parameters to maximize the alignment between its post-adaptation confidence distribution on unlabeled target data and a 'well-calibrated' target distribution, as inspired by Ananya's prior work on relative confidence estimation. We will meta-train on a diverse suite of tasks (e.g., GLUE variants, DomainNet subsets) and evaluate on held-out target domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel meta-learning algorithm for unsupervised fast adaptation, reducing reliance on target labels. It will provide new insights into using model confidence as a self-supervised signal for guiding meta-optimization. The resulting method will be empirically validated on established NLP and vision benchmarks, demonstrating state-of-the-art adaptation efficiency.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in integrating confidence-based meta-learning objectives for purely unsupervised adaptation, moving beyond reconstruction or contrastive losses. It uniquely leverages the model's own confidence preferences—a concept from the researcher's prior work—as the core driver for meta-optimization across domains.

proposals.title: Confidence-Guided Self-Training at Scale: Adapting Models with Web-Scale Unlabeled Corpora
proposals.motivation: While large pre-trained models are foundational, adapting them to specific domains or tasks often requires costly, high-quality labeled data. The internet provides a vast, free, but noisy source of unlabeled text and multimodal data. Leveraging this for efficient adaptation without human annotation is a critical open challenge in machine learning, especially for scaling to diverse real-world applications where labeled data is scarce.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model's own confidence estimates, used to filter and weight examples from a massive, noisy unlabeled corpus, can guide a self-training process that yields more robust and generalizable adaptation than standard fine-tuning on limited labeled data.
proposals.method: We will extend Ananya Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation to develop a confidence-aware self-training algorithm. A base model (e.g., a large language or vision-language model) will generate pseudo-labels and confidence scores for a web-scale corpus (e.g., a filtered Common Crawl snapshot). We will design a curriculum where data is sampled proportionally to confidence, and the model is fine-tuned iteratively. We will experiment with confidence calibration techniques and contrastive losses to prevent shortcut learning and overfitting to noisy pseudo-labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable framework for model adaptation using unlabeled web data, reducing dependency on curated datasets. It will advance understanding of how confidence estimation interacts with self-training at scale and deliver empirical results on benchmark adaptation tasks demonstrating improved robustness and out-of-distribution generalization.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of principled, model-internal confidence estimation—rather than simple entropy thresholds—to dynamically curate a training curriculum from noisy web-scale data, directly building on Kumar's expertise in confidence and shortcut learning avoidance.

proposals.title: Robustness via Synthetic Perturbations: Pre-hardening Vision-Language Models with Generated Distributional Stressors
proposals.motivation: Pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) are brittle to distribution shifts, often relying on shortcut features. Current robustness methods like fine-tuning on target data are reactive and require access to specific shift data. A proactive, general strategy to induce robustness *before* deployment, without needing target domain samples, is a critical open problem in pre-2026 robust machine learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Generating synthetic, unlabeled data that systematically perturbs known model vulnerabilities (e.g., via texture, background, or linguistic style) and using it for self-supervised pre-hardening will improve a model's robustness to a wide range of unforeseen distribution shifts, as measured by out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, leveraging Ananya Kumar's prior work on confidence estimation, we will use a frozen base VLM (e.g., CLIP) to identify low-confidence or high-entropy regions in a generic unlabeled dataset. Second, we will employ a controllable image generator (e.g., a fine-tuned diffusion model) and a large language model to synthesize adversarial variants of these points, guided by a taxonomy of common corruptions and spurious correlations. The original and synthetic data will be used for contrastive self-supervised fine-tuning (e.g., using SimCLR or a custom confidence-aware loss) to 'pre-harden' the model before any downstream task.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for proactive robustness via generative data augmentation, 2) a method to use model confidence to guide the synthesis of 'stress-test' data, and 3) empirical evidence on benchmarks like ImageNet-C, -R, and -A showing improved zero-shot and few-shot OOD performance without target-domain adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Unlike adversarial training or data augmentation on existing shifts, we generate *novel* synthetic shifts to exploit pre-identified model weaknesses. Unlike test-time adaptation, our method is applied once, pre-deployment, making it efficient and broadly applicable.

proposals.title: Continual Confidence Calibration: Online Adaptation of Language Models to Unlabeled Data Streams
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) deployed in real-world settings encounter continuous, non-stationary streams of unlabeled data, leading to distribution shift and degraded performance over time. Current adaptation methods rely on periodic, costly batch retraining with labeled data, which is infeasible for never-ending streams. This work addresses the critical need for efficient, online self-supervised adaptation to maintain model reliability in dynamic environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an LLM's internal confidence signals, estimated via pairwise confidence preferences, can be leveraged to drive a stable, entropy-minimizing online learning process, enabling effective adaptation to a continual stream of unlabeled target data without catastrophic forgetting.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel online algorithm that processes data in small, sequential batches. For each batch, we will compute a self-supervised loss based on the model's relative confidence estimates for perturbed versions of the input, building on Kumar's prior work on confidence preferences. This loss will be combined with a sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) regularizer to stabilize learning and a replay buffer of confident pseudo-labels to mitigate forgetting. The core update will use an online version of stochastic gradient descent with a constrained step size.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new, practical framework for the continual online adaptation of LLMs. It will advance understanding of how internal confidence measures can guide unsupervised learning. The resulting methods will be validated on simulated non-stationary text streams, providing benchmarks for future research in lifelong learning for NLP.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of relative confidence estimation—a discriminative, calibration-aware signal—as the primary driver for online entropy minimization, moving beyond standard self-training or consistency losses which are prone to confirmation bias on non-i.i.d. streams.

proposals.title: Confidence-Guided Data Acquisition: Using Model Uncertainty to Drive Efficient Unlabeled Data Collection for Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Adapting large language models (LLMs) to new domains or tasks often requires costly, targeted data collection. Traditional active learning assumes a fixed, pre-existing unlabeled pool, which is unrealistic for many real-world applications where data must be actively sourced or generated. This work addresses the critical, underexplored problem of how a model itself can guide the strategic acquisition of new, unlabeled data from a vast potential space, moving beyond selection from a static set.
proposals.hypothesis: A model's internal confidence estimates, particularly relative confidence preferences as explored in Kumar's prior work, can be used to formulate an effective acquisition function that identifies the most valuable regions of data space to sample for efficient downstream adaptation via fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage framework. First, we extend relative confidence estimation methods to score not just given examples, but to define a query strategy for a data generator or search API. The acquisition function will target points where the model exhibits high predictive entropy or where its confidence preferences between candidate completions are most ambiguous. Second, we integrate this with a curiosity-driven exploration module, inspired by intrinsic motivation in RL, to occasionally sample high-uncertainty, out-of-distribution prompts. The collected data will then be used for fine-tuning, and efficiency will be benchmarked against baselines using random acquisition and standard pool-based active learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for cost-effective data collection for LLM adaptation, formalizing the link between confidence calibration and external data acquisition. It will yield empirical results on adaptation benchmarks demonstrating superior data efficiency and a publicly released framework for confidence-guided data collection.
proposals.novelty: While active learning selects from a pool, this work uses model confidence to actively *define* what data to collect from an open-world environment. It uniquely combines Kumar's expertise in confidence estimation with the practical problem of guiding data collection pipelines, a shift from passive selection to active guidance.

proposals.title: Self-Calibrating Classifiers: Guaranteed Confidence Calibration Without a Held-Out Set
proposals.motivation: Modern machine learning models, especially large neural networks, are often poorly calibrated, producing confidence scores that do not reflect true probabilities. Current calibration techniques, like temperature scaling or Platt scaling, require a separate, labeled calibration dataset, which is costly to obtain and reduces data available for training. This creates a significant practical bottleneck for deploying reliable models in high-stakes domains.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model can be trained to be inherently self-calibrating by jointly optimizing for task performance and a novel, training-data-only calibration loss, providing formal calibration guarantees without needing any held-out data.
proposals.method: We will develop a training framework that adds a regularization term to the standard cross-entropy loss. This term will minimize a differentiable upper bound on the Expected Calibration Error (ECE) computed solely on the training batch, inspired by techniques like confidence penalty and distribution-aware losses. We will specifically adapt the 'confidence preferences' concept from Kumar's prior work to construct a loss that penalizes over- and under-confidence relative to the empirical training distribution. Theoretical analysis will derive PAC-style guarantees for the in-training calibration error generalizing to the test set.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical method for training well-calibrated models using all available labeled data, eliminating the need for a separate calibration set. It will contribute a new theoretical understanding of in-training calibration and offer a software tool for practitioners to build more trustworthy AI systems.
proposals.novelty: Unlike post-hoc calibration methods, our approach integrates calibration directly into training. The key novelty is providing calibration guarantees using only the training data, a problem largely unaddressed in pre-2026 literature which assumes a held-out set is available.

proposals.title: HELM-Live: A Dynamic Benchmarking Framework for Continuous Evaluation of Evolving Language Models
proposals.motivation: The Holistic Evaluation of Language Models (HELM) provides a crucial static snapshot of model capabilities, but the rapid release of new models (e.g., GPT-4, Claude 2, open-source variants) and novel evaluation scenarios (e.g., reasoning, multimodality, long-context) quickly renders static benchmarks obsolete. This creates a gap in our ability to track and compare the evolving distribution of capabilities and failure modes across the field. A continuously updated, living benchmark is necessary for robust, timely, and distributionally-aware assessment.
proposals.hypothesis: A systematic, automated framework for continuously integrating new models and evaluation scenarios into HELM will reveal dynamic, non-stationary trends in model capabilities and relative confidence that are missed by static evaluations, particularly for fine-tuned variants and emerging tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a modular pipeline that: 1) Automatically ingests new model APIs and open-source checkpoints (via Hugging Face) into a standardized evaluation harness. 2) Implements a scenario registry for new tasks and domains (e.g., code generation, bias probes, multilingual QA), extending the core HELM taxonomy. 3) Applies Ananya Kumar's recent work on relative confidence estimation via 'confidence preferences' to score model outputs across this dynamic test set, tracking how confidence and accuracy co-evolve. 4) Employs a scheduled retraining and reporting system to generate periodic performance leaderboards and capability distribution maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a maintained, living benchmark (HELM-Live) and a public dashboard for tracking model evolution. It will yield new insights into how fine-tuning and scaling laws manifest across a dynamically expanding evaluation distribution, and establish methodologies for continuous evaluation in AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work creates static benchmarks, this proposal introduces a continuous integration and evaluation system specifically for language models, directly integrating confidence estimation metrics to analyze not just performance, but the stability and reliability of model predictions over time.

proposals.title: Taxonomizing Model Confidence and Shortcuts in Multilingual and Multimodal Learning
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on model confidence and shortcut learning is predominantly English-centric and unimodal, creating a significant evaluation gap. As large models are deployed globally and must process diverse data types (e.g., text, images), it is critical to understand whether their confidence calibration and reliance on spurious features generalize across languages and modalities. This work addresses the lack of systematic benchmarks for these phenomena in realistic, heterogeneous settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current confidence estimation methods and generative classifiers will exhibit degraded and inconsistent performance in multilingual and multimodal scenarios, revealing language- and modality-specific shortcut solutions not present in English-only, unimodal evaluations.
proposals.method: We will extend Ananya Kumar's existing taxonomized framework for confidence and shortcut analysis to new benchmarks. For multilingual evaluation, we will fine-tune and test models like mT5 and BLOOM on a curated set of classification and generation tasks across typologically diverse languages (e.g., Swahili, Hindi, Mandarin), using datasets like XNLI and XLSum. For multimodal evaluation, we will adapt vision-language models (e.g., CLIP, BLIP-2) using datasets like Multi30K and newly constructed adversarial sets containing cross-modal shortcuts. We will employ relative confidence estimation via confidence preferences and analyze failure modes through the lens of the established taxonomy.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive benchmark for evaluating model confidence and shortcut learning beyond English text. It will provide actionable insights into the cross-lingual and cross-modal transfer limitations of current methods, informing the development of more robust, globally equitable models. The resulting framework and findings will be a resource for auditing model reliability in diverse real-world applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work taxonomizes confidence and shortcuts, no study systematically applies this analysis across the axes of language and modality simultaneously. This proposal uniquely bridges Kumar's work on confidence preferences and generative classifiers with the practical challenges of multilingual and multimodal deployment.

proposals.title: FLYP-Dense: Contrastive Fine-Tuning for Robust Dense Prediction
proposals.motivation: Dense prediction tasks like object detection and segmentation are critical for real-world vision systems, but models often rely on spurious correlations and shortcut features, harming out-of-distribution robustness. Pre-2026, methods like FLYP (Feature Learning with Your own Preferences) introduced a promising contrastive fine-tuning principle for classification, using confidence preferences to steer models towards more generalizable features. Extending this principle to dense prediction remains unexplored, despite its potential to significantly improve model reliability in safety-critical applications like autonomous driving and medical imaging.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a contrastive fine-tuning objective based on relative confidence estimation to dense prediction models will force them to learn more robust, semantically meaningful features, thereby improving their generalization and calibration on out-of-distribution data compared to standard supervised fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the FLYP framework for dense tasks by constructing positive and negative sample pairs for each pixel or region proposal based on model confidence preferences, using a pre-trained detector (e.g., Mask R-CNN or DETR) as a starting point. The core algorithm will involve a contrastive loss that maximizes similarity between features of high-confidence predictions and their augmented versions, while minimizing similarity with features from low-confidence, potentially shortcut-reliant predictions. We will specifically design sampling strategies for bounding boxes and segmentation masks to generate these contrastive pairs, integrating the loss with the standard task-specific losses (e.g., bounding box regression, mask segmentation).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first demonstration and systematic evaluation of confidence-based contrastive fine-tuning for object detection and segmentation. It will deliver new state-of-the-art robust benchmarks on OOD datasets like COCO-C, Cityscapes-C, and cross-dataset generalization tests. The resulting method will be a practical, post-hoc fine-tuning recipe to debias and improve existing dense prediction models.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established for pre-training and FLYP exists for classification, the novel application of confidence-preference contrastive objectives to the structured output space of dense prediction is non-trivial and unexplored. The key innovation lies in formulating and sampling confidence-based contrastive pairs for spatially-correlated predictions.

proposals.title: Beyond Natural Images: Evaluating the Domain Robustness and Confidence Calibration of FLYP on Synthetic and Scientific Pretraining Data
proposals.motivation: Foundation models like FLYP, which unify vision and language, are predominantly pretrained on web-scale natural image-text pairs. This creates a potential domain bias, limiting their applicability to specialized fields like medical imaging, remote sensing, or synthetic data generation. Understanding how such models' performance, generalization, and confidence calibration—a key theme in Kumar's prior work—are affected by non-natural pretraining domains is crucial for building robust, trustworthy multimodal systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pretraining FLYP on curated non-natural image domains (e.g., synthetic renderings, scientific diagrams) will lead to competitive downstream task performance on in-domain data but will reveal systematic shifts in the model's confidence estimates and generalization patterns compared to natural image pretraining, particularly on out-of-distribution tasks.
proposals.method: We will pretrain multiple FLYP model variants from scratch, holding architecture constant, on distinct datasets: 1) a synthetic domain (e.g., ShapeNet renderings with textual descriptions), 2) a scientific domain (e.g., arXiv figure-caption pairs), and 3) a standard natural image corpus (e.g., a subset of LAION) as a baseline. We will fine-tune and evaluate all variants on a suite of downstream tasks spanning natural, synthetic, and scientific image classification and VQA. Performance will be analyzed alongside confidence metrics (e.g., expected calibration error, entropy) using methods from Kumar's prior work on relative confidence estimation to diagnose distributional shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of domain-specific pretraining for vision-language models like FLYP, establishing benchmarks for non-natural domain performance. It will yield new insights into how pretraining data distribution shapes model confidence and generalization, contributing to the fields of robust multimodal learning and model calibration.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is well-studied in vision, systematically varying the *pretraining* domain for a large multimodal model like FLYP and analyzing its impact on confidence calibration is novel. This moves beyond simple performance comparison to a deeper study of learned representations and uncertainty, aligning with Kumar's research themes.

ideas.title: Efficiently Specialized Foundation Ensembles via Compute-Intensive, Data-Conscious Training
ideas.description: This research explores a training framework that uses massive computational resources not to train a single monolithic model on a vast dataset, but to train a coordinated ensemble of smaller, specialized foundation models on a single, meticulously curated, and significantly smaller corpus. The core technical challenge is to architect the training process—potentially through multi-objective optimization, controlled parameter sharing, and diversity-inducing losses—so that the same high-quality data yields fundamentally different, complementary world models and capabilities across the ensemble. This results in a system that is inherently more robust and less prone to single-point failures than a single foundation model, while the data-conscious approach reduces ethical, legal, and environmental costs compared to continual scaling of dataset size.

ideas.title: Compute-Driven Dynamic Data Distillation for Robust Efficiency
ideas.description: This research explores a paradigm where massive compute is used to dynamically synthesize and curate a small, highly informative training dataset. Instead of a static, human-curated small set, the system employs a tight loop of training on a candidate 'distilled' dataset, generating synthetic data to probe model weaknesses, and adversarially validating against a vast, diverse background corpus. The goal is to automate the creation of a minimal dataset that maximizes model robustness and generality, directly addressing the bias and coverage pitfalls of manual curation while maintaining extreme data efficiency.

ideas.title: Ecology-Guided Contrastive Pre-training for Resilient and Diverse AI Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel pre-training paradigm that integrates ecological resilience theory directly into the contrastive learning objective. By framing different data domains or augmentation strategies as distinct 'niches,' the method encourages models to develop specialized yet robust feature representations that are invariant within niches but diverse across them. This ecological pressure, enforced through a modified contrastive loss with domain adaptation constraints, aims to cultivate a population of models with high functional diversity and low redundancy. The resulting AI ecosystem would be more resilient to distribution shifts and adversarial threats, moving beyond mere performance to engineer systemic health and stability from the ground up.

ideas.title: Foundation Models via Targeted Immunization: Training on High-Fidelity Synthetic Data
ideas.description: This research inverts the assumption of broad data training by drawing an analogy from vaccine development. In that field, mRNA vaccines solved the problem of generating a robust immune response by providing a narrow, synthetic blueprint (mRNA for a specific antigen) rather than a broad, attenuated pathogen. The analogous solution is adapted to foundation model training: instead of training on vast, noisy internet-scale data, we propose training models on a narrow, high-quality, or perfectly engineered synthetic dataset that acts as a 'blueprint' for a domain. This 'targeted immunization' approach would involve generating synthetic data that encapsulates the pristine logical structures and desired reasoning patterns of a specific field (e.g., formal mathematics, code generation, or scientific reasoning). The goal is to produce a foundation model with robust, specialized, and highly reliable foundational skills, immunized against the biases and inconsistencies of broad, low-quality data by design.

ideas.title: Narrow-Expert Foundation Models with a Broad-Spectrum Robustness Scaffold
ideas.description: This research investigates foundation models where a primary 'expert' network is trained on a narrow, high-quality, or synthetic data domain for superior in-domain performance. To address the critical weakness of OOD fragility, the system architecturally couples this expert with a separate 'Robustness Scaffold'—a smaller, efficiently trained model exposed to broad, diverse data. This scaffold continuously monitors inputs for OOD signals and, when activated, either informs the expert's reasoning with generalized priors or guides it to a safe, predefined operational mode. The result is a system that maintains the benefits of focused training while achieving the robustness and fail-safe characteristics of broadly trained models.

ideas.title: Genomic Deconvolution: Reconstructing Training Data Populations from Model Outputs
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from population genetics and metagenomics to the inverse problem of extracting training data from AI models. In metagenomics, tools like SourceTracker deconvolve a mixed DNA sample to infer the composition of original source microbial communities by analyzing genetic marker frequencies against references. Analogously, we propose treating a deployed foundation model as a 'mixed sample' where its internal representations and outputs contain blended statistical signatures of its training data 'populations'. The adapted method involves developing deconvolution algorithms that analyze feature co-occurrence patterns, activation statistics, or generation artifacts within the model. By strategically querying the model and comparing the resulting signals to known data distributions or by solving an optimization problem to separate the blended signals, the method aims to audit and recover representative samples that characterize the composition and proportions of the original training datasets, providing a tool for model transparency and data provenance auditing.

ideas.title: A Theoretical and Interdisciplinary Framework for Auditing Self-Supervised Learning Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel, interdisciplinary framework that extends theoretical analysis from contrastive learning to a broader class of self-supervised pre-training methods (e.g., masked modeling, clustering, predictive coding) to predict their specific societal impacts. By formally linking algorithmic properties—such as the type of invariance learned, data compression, and emergent representations—to potential legal risks, ethical failures, and market distortions, it enables proactive, evidence-based governance. This synergy moves beyond post-hoc impact assessment of existing foundation models to create forward-looking tools for policymakers and developers, allowing them to anticipate and mitigate harms before new classes of models are widely deployed.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Stability for High-Precision, Narrow-Domain Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research investigates the design of foundation models trained on narrow, high-quality, or synthetic data domains (e.g., medical protocols, legal reasoning, hardware verification) through the lens of control theory. It formalizes the model's learning dynamics and output generation as a feedback control system, where the curated data domain defines the desired operational envelope. The core synergy is using control-theoretic tools (e.g., Lyapunov stability, robust control) to analyze and ensure the model's stability—meaning bounded, predictable, and non-catastrophic outputs—both during initial training on the narrow domain and during subsequent task-specific adaptations. This enables the development of certifiably stable models for high-stakes applications, where reliability is paramount and broad internet-scale training introduces unacceptable instability risks.

ideas.title: Unifying Theoretical Analysis of Self-Supervised Learning via a Common Dynamical Systems Framework
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending theoretical analysis from contrastive learning to other self-supervised pre-training methods (e.g., masked autoencoding, feature clustering, distillation). Drawing an analogy from theoretical ecology, where diverse species interactions are modeled under a unified generalized Lotka-Volterra framework, the solution is to adapt this approach to machine learning. The adapted idea is to construct a common dynamical systems framework that abstracts the optimization dynamics of various SSL methods, treating their specific mechanisms as parameters within a unified mathematical model. This would enable a comparative theoretical analysis of convergence properties, representation geometry, and learning stability across the entire family of self-supervised learning algorithms, moving beyond method-specific theories.

ideas.title: Immunological Training: Adapting Immune System Principles to Detect and Isolate Malicious Training Data
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from immunology to enhance security and privacy in foundation model training. The analogous problem—how biological systems defend against diverse, evolving pathogens without self-harm—is solved through layered detection, adaptive memory, and self/non-self discrimination. The adapted idea is to develop an 'immunological' training framework for AI. This system would implement innate detection layers for statistical anomalies, adaptive layers that learn signatures of data poisoning and privacy attacks (treating them as 'non-self'), and a memory mechanism to isolate or mitigate the influence of identified malicious data. This bio-inspired approach aims to create models that are dynamically resilient to novel and evolving training data threats, securing the model's integrity and the privacy of its training sources.

ideas.title: A Unified Geometric and Information-Theoretic Framework for Self-Supervised Learning
ideas.description: This research develops a unified theoretical framework to analyze and compare diverse self-supervised learning (SSL) methods, moving beyond isolated case studies. The core approach is to model SSL objectives through a common lens of representation space geometry and information-theoretic constraints, defining universal metrics like feature alignment, uniformity, and rank. This framework enables the derivation of generalization guarantees that hold across method families and provides a principled basis for characterizing the trade-offs between different pretext tasks. The ultimate goal is to establish a foundational theory that explains why various SSL methods work, predicts their performance, and guides the synthesis of new, more effective objectives.

ideas.title: Compositional Foundation Models: A Modular Ecosystem to Counter Homogenization
ideas.description: This research proposes a new paradigm for foundation models where the core model is not a single monolithic network but a dynamic, compositional system of many small, specialized modules. This modular architecture enables the foundation model to maintain and evolve a diverse internal 'ecology' of skills and representations, inherently resisting the homogenization pressure that occurs when a single dense model is fine-tuned for many tasks. Downstream adaptation then becomes a process of selecting, composing, and fine-tuning specific, relevant modules rather than shifting the entire model, preserving the system's overall diversity and robustness. This approach aims to deliver the scale and capability of large models while structurally embedding the benefits of a multi-model ecosystem within a single, composable system.

ideas.title: Property-Based Training Data Audit via Probabilistic Influence and Membership Inference
ideas.description: This research develops methods to audit foundation models for the presence or absence of specific, legally or ethically relevant properties in their training data, without attempting full data reconstruction. It combines advanced membership inference attacks, influence function approximations, and targeted activation analysis to produce statistical evidence that certain data characteristics are over-represented. The outcome is a toolkit that can provide verifiable claims, such as 'this model's training data likely contained copyrighted text from domain X with confidence Y', enabling practical compliance and safety audits.

ideas.title: Adversarial Data Provenance: Using Extraction Attacks to Design and Certify Robust Privacy-Preserving Training
ideas.description: This research direction proposes using state-of-the-art training data extraction and membership inference attacks as a core component of the model development lifecycle. By actively and repeatedly attempting to extract or infer properties of the training data from intermediate model checkpoints during training, we can identify specific vulnerabilities to both privacy leaks and poisoning influences. These insights then directly inform the design of adaptive, hybrid defense mechanisms—such as refined differential privacy budgets, data sanitization, or robust aggregation—that are empirically validated against the very extraction techniques they aim to thwart. The outcome is a rigorous framework for producing foundation models with verifiable, attack-aware guarantees of privacy and integrity, where the defensive measures are proven effective against the most potent known extraction-based audits.

ideas.title: Econometric Causal Frameworks for the Macro-Effects of Self-Supervised Learning Paradigms
ideas.description: This research develops a novel econometric framework to measure and compare the causal economic impacts of different self-supervised pre-training paradigms (e.g., contrastive, masked autoencoding, diffusion) used in foundation models. By integrating theoretical insights from SSL—such as the data efficiency, representational biases, and task generalization properties of each paradigm—into structural econometric models, it seeks to isolate how each architectural choice propagates through the economy. This enables counterfactual analysis, predicting how labor demand, productivity growth, and industry disruption might differ if the dominant pre-training paradigm were to shift, providing a rigorous, causal tool for policymakers and firms to anticipate the macroeconomic consequences of advances in AI theory.

ideas.title: Stability-Preserving Control for Extreme Task-Specific Distillation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates control-theoretic stability analysis directly into the extreme knowledge distillation pipeline. The goal is to not only compress a large model's capability for a single task into a tiny model but to design the distillation loss and architecture to satisfy formal stability criteria, such as Lyapunov or bounded-input bounded-output stability, for the specialist model's input-output mapping. This ensures the distilled model is robust to distribution shifts, adversarial inputs, and fine-tuning on edge data, which are critical for deployment. The outcome is a new class of certifiably stable, ultra-efficient specialist models that are reliable enough for safety-critical applications.

ideas.title: High-Resolution Computational Microscopy for Model Training
ideas.description: This idea explores training paradigms that apply massive compute to carefully curated, smaller datasets, drawing an analogy from structural biology's Cryo-Electron Microscopy (Cryo-EM). In Cryo-EM, researchers use enormous computational power on a relatively small set of noisy 2D projection images to reconstruct high-resolution 3D atomic models through sophisticated iterative algorithms, rather than simply collecting more raw data. Adapted to machine learning, this suggests a paradigm where training is treated as a high-resolution reconstruction problem. Instead of scaling data volume, massive compute is directed toward intensive, iterative processing of a small, ultra-curated 'specimen' dataset. The research would develop novel training algorithms that perform deep analysis on each data point—extracting maximum signal, learning robust representations through synthetic perturbations and multi-view analysis, and reconstructing the underlying data manifold—to achieve superior generalization and efficiency from a minimal set of high-quality examples.

ideas.title: Epigenetic Distillation: Compressing Foundation Models via Task-Specific Regulatory Pruning
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from epigenetics in developmental biology, where a single genome gives rise to diverse, specialized cell types through regulatory silencing and activation, this research adapts the concept to model compression. The analogous solution uses epigenetic markers to create efficient specialists from a full blueprint. The adapted idea proposes 'epigenetic distillation': first, train a regulatory attention mask on the large foundation model to permanently deactivate neurons and pathways irrelevant to a single target task while strengthening the critical subcircuit. This creates a sparse, task-purified version of the large model. This sparse model is then used as the teacher for extreme distillation into a tiny student model, ensuring the compression captures only the essential, activated computational graph for that specialty, mimicking how a liver cell operates using only a specific subset of the genome's instructions.

ideas.title: Ensembles of Specialized Foundational Components for Stable and Diverse AI Systems
ideas.description: This research direction proposes moving beyond the pursuit of a single, maximally adaptable foundation model. Instead, it advocates for designing and training an ensemble of smaller, more specialized foundational components, each optimized for stability, safety, and verifiability within a specific domain or capability class. These components are integrated through a structured compositional framework that routes tasks to the appropriate specialized module, deliberately relaxing the need for any single component to be universally adaptable. This approach directly mitigates homogenization risk by introducing architectural diversity at the foundation level, reduces systemic failure modes by containing issues within specialized components, and enhances overall system safety by allowing for deeper, more focused verification of each stable part.

ideas.title: Adapting Pharmaceutical Stress Testing to Create Adversarial Failure Mode Benchmarks for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the systematic stress testing methodology from pharmaceutical development (specifically ICH stability testing guidelines) to the evaluation of foundation models. In pharmaceuticals, stress testing exposes products to extreme environmental conditions (heat, light, humidity, pH) to force degradation, identify failure modes, and establish safe operating boundaries. The adapted approach involves creating a benchmark suite that subjects foundation models to analogous 'extreme but plausible' input stressors: systematic semantic perturbations, logical contradictions, distributional shifts, and adversarial prompts outside their standard training distribution. By forcing failures in a controlled manner, the research aims to systematically catalog model failure modes, analyze the 'breakdown products' (e.g., specific types of incoherence, bias amplification, or harmful content generation), and ultimately establish clear operational boundaries and safety margins for real-world deployment, moving beyond average-case performance to worst-case robustness.

ideas.title: Causal Attribution of Model Impacts via Training Data Provenance
ideas.description: This research develops a novel methodology that combines data extraction techniques from foundation models with econometric causal inference models. The core idea is to first audit and recover representative samples or statistical fingerprints of specific training data domains (e.g., code repositories, customer service transcripts, scientific literature). These recovered data signatures are then used as instrumental variables or treatment identifiers in quasi-experimental econometric models, allowing researchers to isolate and quantify the causal impact of deploying models trained on that specific data. This enables precise attribution of economic outcomes—such as labor market shifts, productivity changes, or innovation rates—to the influence of particular data sources within the model's training corpus, providing a powerful tool for policy and accountability.

ideas.title: Efficient Emergence via Biological Network Principles
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that scale (parameter count, data volume) is the only path to emergent capabilities in AI. Drawing an analogy from systems biology and neuroscience, where complex cognition emerges from efficient, specialized local circuits rather than sheer scale of neurons, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea is to design and test smaller-scale neural architectures that incorporate principles of biological efficiency—such as sparse, modular, and recurrent connectivity patterns, dynamic resource allocation, and task-specific specialization. The goal is to determine whether these architectural innovations, focused on the 'quality' of connections and computation, can trigger emergent reasoning, generalization, or other advanced capabilities in models with orders-of-magnitude fewer parameters, providing a more sustainable and interpretable alternative to scaling laws.

ideas.title: Anchored Dual-Expert: Robust Specialization via Generalist Anchor Distillation
ideas.description: This research addresses the brittleness of extreme single-task distillation by co-distilling a foundation model's capabilities into a pair of small, complementary networks. A tiny, task-specialized 'expert' model is distilled for peak efficiency on the target task, while a separate, slightly larger 'anchor' model is distilled to preserve a broad slice of the foundation model's general knowledge and reasoning. A trainable gating mechanism learns to combine their outputs, ensuring the system defaults to the anchor's robust understanding when inputs deviate from the specialist's core competence, thereby maintaining reliability without sacrificing primary task performance.

ideas.title: Automated Adversarial Failure Discovery for Proactive Model Auditing
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop system that automatically discovers and characterizes novel failure modes in foundation models, moving beyond static benchmarks. It employs meta-learned adversaries and environment simulators to generate evolving, targeted challenges that stress the model in ways human designers would not anticipate. The system produces not just a performance score but a structured 'failure atlas' that maps the discovered vulnerabilities to model internals and data characteristics. This enables proactive robustness auditing and guides targeted improvements before failures manifest in real-world deployments.

ideas.title: Formal Failure Mode Specification and Verification for Foundational Models
ideas.description: This research direction integrates systematic empirical evaluation with formal verification to create a new paradigm for model safety. It develops methods to translate observed failure modes from adversarial or out-of-distribution benchmarks into formal, machine-checkable specifications of undesirable behavior. These specifications then guide the application of formal methods (e.g., abstract interpretation, model checking) to verify that a model's architecture or training process provably avoids these specific, empirically-grounded failure patterns. The synergy lies in the feedback loop: verification results that reveal specification gaps or verification limits drive the creation of more targeted and challenging evaluation benchmarks, leading to a cycle of progressively stronger, evidence-based safety assurances.

ideas.title: Critical Phase Transitions in Minimal Neural Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts the study of critical phenomena from statistical physics to the problem of finding minimal scales for emergent capabilities in AI. In physics, the minimal conditions for a phase transition are identified by reducing system size and interaction strength to find the critical point where an order parameter shows a sharp change. Similarly, we propose to systematically traverse the (N, D) scaling plane for language models, measuring a specific capability (e.g., multi-step reasoning) as our order parameter. The goal is to identify the critical scaling frontier—the smallest model and dataset combination—where this capability first manifests as a discontinuous jump in performance, thereby defining a minimal 'emergent capability model' analogous to a system at its critical point.

ideas.title: Applying Financial Portfolio Theory to Diversify AI Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from financial portfolio management to the problem of risk in homogenized AI ecosystems. In finance, Modern Portfolio Theory provides a quantitative framework to mitigate systemic risk by constructing a diversified portfolio of assets with uncorrelated returns. Similarly, we can model an AI ecosystem—comprising various models, training pipelines, and hardware—as a portfolio where 'risk' is the probability of widespread, cascading failure (e.g., from a common architectural flaw or data poisoning attack), and 'return' is aggregate performance. The adapted research would develop methods to measure the correlation of failure modes and performance shocks across different AI system components. Using these metrics, we can apply portfolio optimization techniques to guide the development and deployment of AI systems, intentionally engineering for diversity in architectures, training data, and objectives to build ecosystem-level resilience, thereby reducing systemic risk while maintaining high collective capability.

ideas.title: Foundation Models as High-Reliability Systems: A Control Theory Approach
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from control systems engineering, this idea challenges the assumption that foundation models must be optimized primarily for maximum task adaptability. In high-stakes control systems (like aviation or nuclear power), engineers prioritize stability and safety within a defined operating envelope over raw performance or agility. The analogous solution involves designing controllers that robustly reject disturbances and maintain operation within safe bounds, even if it means not exploiting the system's full theoretical capability. Adapted to AI, this means building foundation models where the core training objective is to establish and remain within a mathematically defined 'stable operating envelope' for behavior. The model is optimized for low variance, predictable degradation, and safety-guaranteed outputs, rather than for peak few-shot performance on arbitrary novel tasks. This could involve new architectural constraints and loss functions that penalize trajectories in latent space that lead outside the verified safe region, fundamentally trading off some breadth of adaptability for guaranteed stability and safety.

ideas.title: Sparse, Hierarchically-Routed Mixture of Experts with a Dense Integrative Core
ideas.description: This architecture scales capability by composing many small, specialized expert modules, but directly solves the routing bottleneck through a two-tier system. A trainable hierarchical router efficiently activates a sparse, task-relevant subset of experts for any input, drastically reducing computational overhead compared to naive modular approaches. A persistent, dense core model of moderate size is co-trained to integrate the outputs of these activated experts, maintaining strong cross-domain knowledge synthesis. This design captures the benefits of modular specialization and parameter efficiency while preserving the inference speed and integrative capacity of a unified network.

ideas.title: Theoretical Distillation: Using Emergence Theory to Guide the Compression of Emergent Capabilities
ideas.description: This research fuses the study of emergent capabilities with extreme distillation to create a novel, theory-driven compression paradigm. The core idea is to use theoretical frameworks that explain *why* and *how* capabilities emerge at scale to inform *how* they can be algorithmically disentangled and distilled. Instead of treating the large model as a black-box source of capability, its internal mechanistic structure—as predicted or explained by emergence theory—guides the distillation process. This enables the creation of ultra-efficient specialist models that not only perform a task but also provably embody the specific emergent reasoning or representation pathway, making them more interpretable, reliable, and fundamentally different from standard knowledge-distilled models.

ideas.title: Control-Theoretic Co-Design for Stable Emergence in Efficient Architectures
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the emergent capabilities observed in massive models can be systematically engineered in smaller, more efficient architectures through the co-design of the model architecture and its training dynamics using control theory. Instead of merely analyzing stability post-hoc, we propose using control-theoretic principles (e.g., Lyapunov stability, adaptive control) to actively shape the learning trajectory, guiding the model through phases of rapid capability acquisition while maintaining output stability and preventing catastrophic forgetting during fine-tuning. This approach aims to discover architectural and algorithmic 'sweet spots' where efficiency and stable emergence are not trade-offs but co-designed properties, potentially enabling a new class of robust, capable, and resource-efficient foundation models.

ideas.title: Formally-Verified, Evolvable Stability via Managed Adaptation Boundaries
ideas.description: This approach constructs a foundation model with a dual architecture: a highly stable, verified core optimized for safety and a set of explicitly defined, managed adaptation interfaces. The system continuously monitors its performance and the environment for signs of distribution shift or legitimate new task demands. When change is required, proposed adaptations are confined to a sandbox and subjected to formal verification and rigorous safety testing against the core's stability criteria. Only adaptations that provably maintain the system's safety and stability properties are integrated, enabling controlled evolution without sacrificing the original guarantees.

ideas.title: Cryptographically Verifiable and Formally Constrained Training for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research develops a framework that embeds security and privacy guarantees directly into the foundation model training process through cryptographic and formal methods. It uses verifiable data structures and commitment schemes to create an immutable, publicly auditable ledger of all training data provenance and transformations. Simultaneously, it employs formal specification and runtime verification to constrain the training algorithm's behavior, ensuring it adheres to strict safety and privacy properties. This approach moves beyond patching specific attacks to providing foundational, mathematically-grounded assurances of training integrity and data governance.

ideas.title: Cellular Differentiation for Model Scaling: A Developmental Biology Approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of cellular differentiation from developmental biology to solve the problem of scaling AI model capability. The analogous field is Developmental Biology/Embryogenesis, where a single zygote scales into a complex organism not through monolithic growth but via modular specialization—stem cells differentiate into distinct tissues and organs that cooperate. The analogous solution is this process of hierarchical, lineage-specific commitment from a common blueprint. Adapted to AI research, the idea proposes a 'developmental' scaling paradigm. We begin with a foundational 'stem' model. Through structured training phases, we induce functional differentiation within the parameter space, creating many small, specialized modules (e.g., for logic, sensory processing, knowledge access). A lightweight, learned routing network then dynamically composes these modules for each task, scaling overall capability by increasing modular diversity and efficient composition, not just raw parameter count.

ideas.title: The Efficiency Budget: A Formal Framework for Challenging Scaling Laws with Architectural Innovation
ideas.description: This research establishes a controlled experimental framework to directly test whether architectural efficiency can rival scale. It defines a strict 'Efficiency Budget'—a fixed computational cost per inference—and within this budget, develops and trains novel, highly efficient architectures (e.g., based on state-space models, mixture-of-experts, or algorithmic improvements). Their performance on a curated benchmark of 'emergent ability' tasks is then compared against baseline models (like transformers) scaled down to fit the same budget. The goal is to produce quantifiable evidence of whether architectural innovation can create a new Pareto frontier, challenging the assumption that scaling parameters and data is the only path to advanced capabilities.

ideas.title: A Portfolio Approach to Foundation Models: Engineering Diversity to Mitigate Systemic Risk
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that model homogenization is an inevitable outcome of scaling. It draws an analogy from financial portfolio theory, where systemic risk is mitigated by holding a diversified mix of non-correlated assets. The analogous solution is to construct a portfolio designed to avoid a single point of failure. Adapted to AI, the idea is to engineer a diverse ecosystem of specialized foundation models as a deliberate risk-management strategy. This involves researching methods to quantify 'model correlation' across different architectures and training regimes, and developing orchestration systems that treat this diverse portfolio as a unified, resilient resource. The goal is to create a framework where strategic diversity, not convergence, is optimized for overall system safety and performance.

ideas.title: Adapt Pharmacoepidemiology's Causal Inference Toolkit for AI Impact Measurement
ideas.description: This research adapts the causal inference toolkit from pharmacoepidemiology—a field that rigorously assesses drug effects from real-world observational data—to the problem of measuring the economic impact of foundation models. In pharmacoepidemiology, researchers employ designs like cohort studies and methods like propensity score matching and instrumental variables to estimate causal effects despite confounding variables and the ethical/practical impossibility of randomized controlled trials for many exposures. Similarly, the deployment of foundation models across the economy is a large-scale, quasi-natural experiment, not a randomized trial. The adapted idea treats AI model adoption as an 'exposure' and economic/labor metrics as 'outcomes,' using these established causal inference techniques on observational data (e.g., firm-level adoption data linked to employment and productivity statistics) to quantify the specific, causal impact of AI deployment, controlling for other concurrent economic shifts.

ideas.title: Architecting Decentralized Foundation Model Ecosystems for Inherent Systemic Resilience
ideas.description: This research moves beyond patching single-model risks by designing the technical and socio-economic frameworks for a resilient, multi-model ecosystem. It investigates methods to incentivize and sustain the development of architecturally diverse foundation models trained on distinct data, preventing centralized monoculture. Core challenges include creating robust interoperability standards, cross-model auditing for complementary strengths and failure modes, and economic models that reward diversity over winner-take-all consolidation. The outcome is a blueprint for a pluralistic AI infrastructure where systemic risks are inherently reduced through decentralization and heterogeneity.

ideas.title: Applying Lyapunov Stability Analysis from Dynamical Systems to Foundation Model Fine-Tuning
ideas.description: This idea adapts Lyapunov stability analysis from nonlinear dynamical systems and control engineering to ensure the stability of foundation models. The analogous solution constructs a Lyapunov function to prove an equilibrium point's stability by showing an energy-like measure consistently decreases. Adapted to AI, we define a computable metric that acts as a Lyapunov function for the model's behavior in output space. During fine-tuning or sequential generation, we analyze the trend of this function, and the optimization process or a lightweight controller is constrained to ensure it decreases, thereby guaranteeing the model's outputs converge to a desired, stable regime and preventing instability from updates or feedback loops.

ideas.title: Minimal Viable Impact Models: Interdisciplinary Benchmarks for Societal Risk and Benefit
ideas.description: This research program fuses the search for minimal emergent capability models with interdisciplinary impact studies to establish a new class of benchmarks. The core objective is to identify the smallest model scale and simplest training data required for a capability to first manifest in a way that has measurable legal, ethical, or economic consequence. By creating these 'minimal viable impact models' in collaboration with domain experts from law, economics, and ethics, the project generates concrete, interpretable test cases for analyzing mechanisms of societal risk and benefit. This enables proactive, evidence-based governance frameworks and safety testing protocols tailored to specific capability thresholds, rather than reacting to the full complexity of largest-scale systems.

ideas.title: Koopman-Based Stability Manifolds for Provable Foundation Model Control
ideas.description: This approach addresses the high-dimensionality of foundation models by learning a stable, low-dimensional manifold that captures the essential dynamics of model behavior. Using tools from Koopman operator theory, it identifies linear embeddings from non-linear model rollouts, enabling the application of classical control-theoretic stability analysis. The fine-tuning process is then constrained to keep the model's evolution within this provably stable manifold, ensuring robust and predictable outputs. This bridges the gap between abstract model internals and tractable stability guarantees.

ideas.title: A Formal Theory of Strong Emergence: Predicting Phase Transitions and Novel Primitives in Scaling AI Models
ideas.description: This research develops a formal, predictive theory of 'strong emergence' in foundation models, moving beyond descriptive scaling laws. It defines emergence as the appearance of novel computational primitives or irreducible capabilities at scale, characterized by sharp phase transitions in performance metrics and complexity measures. The framework integrates tools from statistical mechanics to model these transitions and employs systematic ablation studies to isolate and verify emergent mechanisms. The goal is to produce a falsifiable theory that can predict *which* new capabilities will emerge, at *what* scale, and *why*, based on architectural and data properties.

ideas.title: Adapting Hardware Circuit Verification to Neural Network Safety
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from digital hardware verification, where formal methods like model checking are used to prove temporal and logical properties of circuit designs before manufacturing. The analogous solution involves modeling the circuit as a finite-state machine and exhaustively verifying specified properties written in temporal logic. Adapted to foundation model safety, the research would involve abstracting critical behavioral aspects of a model into a verifiable state-transition system. The goal is to define precise safety properties (e.g., concerning harmful content generation or privacy violations) in a formal logic language, and then develop or apply verification tools to analyze the model's parameters and architecture to provide mathematical proof that these properties hold under all specified input conditions, thereby offering strong, pre-deployment safety guarantees.

ideas.title: Establish a Translational Impact Framework for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the successful 'translational pipeline' model from medicine to address the challenge of ensuring foundation models achieve positive societal impact. In translational medicine, the T1-T4 framework systematically moves discoveries from basic research to clinical practice with integrated safety and efficacy checks. By creating an analogous 'Translational Impact Framework' for AI, we can structure the development and deployment of foundation models into defined phases (e.g., basic research, controlled environment testing, limited pilot, full societal deployment). At each phase, interdisciplinary teams—integrating law, ethics, economics, and technical AI research—would be required to collaboratively conduct and pass assessments of potential legal risks, ethical implications, and economic disruptions. This institutionalizes deep, ongoing collaboration across fields and embeds societal impact analysis directly into the AI development lifecycle, transforming it from a retrospective audit to a proactive, parallel design process.

ideas.title: A Statistical Mechanics Framework for Predicting Phase Transitions in Model Capabilities
ideas.description: This research adapts the theoretical framework of phase transitions from statistical physics to predict and explain emergent capabilities in foundation models. The analogous problem—predicting sudden qualitative changes in a complex system's behavior from continuous changes in underlying parameters—was solved in physics using order parameters, scaling laws, and renormalization group theory. The adapted idea treats model scale (parameters, data, compute) as control parameters and defines quantitative 'capability order parameters' to measure the sharpness of new skill acquisition. The goal is to create a theoretical framework that uses scaling laws to predict the critical scales at which new capabilities phase in, and employs concepts akin to renormalization to explain how representations and computational pathways reorganize at these emergent thresholds, moving beyond descriptive scaling laws to a mechanistic theory of emergence.

ideas.title: Orchestrated Heterogeneity: A Meta-Coordination Framework for Specialized Foundation Model Ecosystems
ideas.description: This research designs a meta-architecture to make a diverse ecosystem of specialized foundation models functionally coherent and economically viable. It develops a standardized interaction protocol for model discovery, capability advertising, and task handoff, alongside a lightweight meta-coordination layer that manages dynamic composition and conflict resolution. The system ensures that specialization does not lead to fragmentation by guaranteeing interoperability and reducing integration costs. This approach preserves resilience against single points of failure while enabling the ecosystem to act as a unified, adaptable, and efficient super-system.

ideas.title: Formally Verified Societal Contracts for AI Systems
ideas.description: This research direction establishes a new interdisciplinary practice to codify high-level legal, ethical, and economic principles for AI into precise, formal specifications. Computer scientists and formal methods experts then work directly with legal scholars, ethicists, and economists to develop verification techniques that can prove a foundation model's behavior adheres to these composite 'societal contracts.' This moves beyond proving narrow technical safety to demonstrating alignment with enforceable, multifaceted societal values. The result is a rigorous, auditable bridge between human-centered governance and computational guarantee, creating systems whose societal impacts are verifiably bounded by design.

ideas.title: Societal Decision-Point Engineering: Co-Creating Tested Governance Artifacts for Foundation Models
ideas.description: This research program starts not from disciplines but from concrete, high-consequence societal decision-points involving foundation models, such as regulatory approval for deployment or the drafting of liability clauses. Interdisciplinary teams—including legal scholars, ethicists, economists, and engineers—are formed around each decision-point with the explicit mandate to design, prototype, and stress-test a specific governance artifact. The research rigorously evaluates these artifacts (e.g., compliance checklists, simulation models for economic externalities, standardized audit reports) through iterative workshops with real decision-makers, ensuring they are actionable, legally robust, and economically informed. Success is measured by the artifact's adoption in pilot governance processes and its ability to structure complex interdisciplinary trade-offs into auditable decisions.

ideas.title: Causal Micro-Experiments and Structural Synthesis for AI Labor Impact
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond passive econometric observation by actively generating causal evidence through designed micro-experiments. It partners with firms to run staggered RCTs, measuring the direct effect of foundation model tools on specific job tasks and productivity. These experimental results are then used to calibrate a mechanism-rich structural model of the labor market, which simulates economy-wide impacts by modeling worker skill adaptation, firm adoption dynamics, and occupational task re-bundling. This synthesis of experimental micro-data and theory-grounded simulation provides a robust, causally-informed assessment of economic impacts, overcoming the fundamental counterfactual problem of purely observational studies.
